topics.title: Familiarize Linux networking commands (ifconfig, ping, traceroute, netstat, nmap, tcpdump, etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL507 - NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarize Linux networking commands (ifconfig, ping, traceroute, netstat, nmap, tcpdump, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Computer Networks Laboratory (PCCSL507)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, *Experiment 2 — Familiarize Linux Networking Commands* is a foundational practical session designed to introduce students to the standard **command-line diagnostic and administrative utilities** available in Unix/Linux operating systems for **inspecting, configuring, and troubleshooting** TCP/IP networks.

These utilities leverage the **Berkeley Sockets API** and the Linux **netlink** subsystem to communicate directly with the kernel's networking stack, allowing the operator to view interface state, test reachability, trace routes, capture live packets, and enumerate open services on remote hosts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PCCSL507):**
> *This experiment directly addresses **Course Outcome CO1**: "Implement networking concepts using simulation tools and network programming". The session provides the prerequisite diagnostic vocabulary used in every subsequent experiment (Wireshark, NS2/NS3, socket programming).*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Note (Examiner's Favourite):** Before the `iproute2` suite, `ifconfig` (from `net-tools`) was the *de facto* tool. Since **2019**, major Linux distributions (Ubuntu, Debian, RHEL) have **deprecated** `ifconfig`. Modern scripts use `ip`, `ss`, and `traceroute` from the `iputils` and `iproute2` packages. Always mention this transition in viva!

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **mechanic working on a car (the network)**:

- **`ifconfig / ip addr`** → Looking at the **dashboard instruments** (fuel gauge, speedometer). It tells you *who you are* on the road — your IP, your MAC, your interfaces.
- **`ping`** → Honking at a car ahead to confirm *“Are you there?”*. It sends a tiny beep and waits for an echo.
- **`traceroute`** → Dropping **numbered flags** at every intersection on a highway to find out which roads your car actually travelled.
- **`netstat / ss`** → Reading the **call log** of a switchboard — every active call, every line open, every caller waiting.
- **`nmap`** → Shining a **flashlight into every window** of a building to see which rooms are lit (services running on ports).
- **`tcpdump`** → Tapping a **wiretap** onto the phone line and *recording* every single conversation, byte for byte.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for viva:** "**I P T N N T**" → *Interface, Ping, Trace, Netstat, Nmap, Tcpdump*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU-Recognised)

The following RFC-defined constants govern the behaviour of every networking command in this experiment:

| Metric / Constant | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Default TTL (Linux) | **64** | RFC 791 |
| Default TTL (Windows) | **128** | Microsoft |
| Default ICMP Echo Data Size | **56 bytes** (payload) | RFC 792 |
| Default ping interval | **1 second** | Linux man-page |
| MTU for Ethernet | **1500 bytes** | IEEE 802.3 |
| Loopback address | **127.0.0.1** | RFC 1122 |
| Port range 0–1023 | **Well-known / privileged** | IANA |
| Port range 1024–49151 | **Registered** | IANA |
| Port range 49152–65535 | **Dynamic / Ephemeral** | IANA |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Command Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Categories

The Linux networking command ecosystem is logically grouped into **six functional pillars**. Each command sits inside a pillar based on *what* it interrogates inside the **OSI / TCP-IP stack**.

### Pillar A — Interface & Address Configuration
Commands that talk to **Layer 2 / Layer 3** of the local node.

- **`ifconfig`** *(legacy, deprecated)* — Shows/sets IP, netmask, broadcast, MAC, MTU of interfaces.
- **`ip addr`** *(modern replacement)* — `ip addr show` displays all addresses; `ip addr add 192.168.1.10/24 dev eth0` assigns.
- **`ip link`** — Operates at Layer 2 (MAC, MTU, state UP/DOWN).
- **`ip route`** — Shows/manipulates the routing table.
- **`hostname`**, **`hostname -I`** — Display the system's hostname and all assigned IPs.

### Pillar B — Connectivity & Reachability
Commands that test **end-to-end L3 connectivity** using the **ICMP** protocol (RFC 792).

- **`ping [host]`** — Sends *ICMP Echo Request*; expects *ICMP Echo Reply*.
- **`ping6`** — IPv6 variant.
- **`traceroute [host]`** — Uses **TTL-exceeded** ICMP messages (UDP by default in Linux) to map every hop.
- **`tracepath`** — Similar to traceroute but does not require root and does not use UDP.
- **`mtr`** — A real-time combination of `ping` + `traceroute` (advanced).

### Pillar C — DNS Resolution Tools
Commands that interrogate the **Domain Name System (DNS)**.

- **`nslookup [host]`** — Query DNS servers; deprecated but still taught.
- **`dig [host]`** *(modern)* — Provides verbose DNS query details (A, MX, NS, TXT, SOA).
- **`host [name]`** — Simple forward/reverse lookup.
- **`whois [domain]`** — Queries the **WHOIS** database for registration info.

### Pillar D — Connection & Socket Inspection
Commands that list **active / listening / established** sockets.

- **`netstat -tulnp`** — Lists **T**CP, **U**DP, **L**istening sockets with the **P**ID and numeric port.
- **`ss -tulnp`** *(modern replacement)* — Faster, more detailed.
- **`netstat -an`** — All connections, no DNS resolution.
- **`netstat -s`** — Protocol-wise statistics.

### Pillar E — Discovery & Scanning
Commands that probe **remote hosts** for open services.

- **`nmap [host]`** — The de-facto *Network Mapper*. Performs host discovery, port scanning, OS fingerprinting, version detection.
- **`arp -a`** — Shows the **ARP table** (IP ↔ MAC resolution on the local LAN).
- **`arping`** — ARP-level ping.

### Pillar F — Packet Capture (Sniffing)
Commands that place the NIC into **promiscuous mode** and record raw frames.

- **`tcpdump -i eth0`** — The classic CLI packet analyser (uses libpcap).
- **`tcpdump -w file.pcap`** — Saves capture to a file (readable by Wireshark).
- **`wireshark`** — GUI-based packet analyser (often paired with tcpdump).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" matters in viva:** A student who says *"ping uses ICMP"* is **average**. A student who says *"ping uses **ICMP Type 8 (Echo Request)** and expects **ICMP Type 0 (Echo Reply)**, and the RTT is measured as the time between sending the request and receiving the reply, where the TTL of the request packet starts at 64 on Linux and is decremented by each router; a destination-unreachable or time-exceeded message replaces echo-reply on failure"* — that student gets **full marks**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since this is a **Lab course**, the "formulas" are the **command invocations and the fields they expose**. The table below is the **single most important reference** for your viva and record-book.

| # | Command | Category | Purpose | Key Flags | Sample Output Field |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ifconfig` | Interface | Show all interfaces | `-a` (all) | `inet`, `netmask`, `ether`, `RX/TX packets` |
| 2 | `ip addr show` | Interface | Show all IPs | `shorthand: ip a` | `inet 192.168.1.5/24` |
| 3 | `ping host` | Connectivity | Test reachability | `-c 4` (count), `-i 2` (interval) | `time=12.3 ms`, `ttl=64` |
| 4 | `traceroute host` | Connectivity | Map path | `-m 30` (max TTL), `-n` (no DNS) | Hop number, RTT ×3, IP |
| 5 | `netstat -tulnp` | Sockets | Listening ports | `-p` (PID), `-n` (numeric) | `Proto Local-Address Foreign-Address State` |
| 6 | `ss -tulnp` | Sockets | Modern netstat | `-l` (listen), `-p` (process) | Faster than netstat |
| 7 | `nslookup host` | DNS | DNS query | `-type=mx` | `Server`, `Address`, `Name` |
| 8 | `dig host` | DNS | Detailed DNS | `+short`, `+trace` | `ANSWER SECTION`, `TTL` |
| 9 | `arp -a` | LAN | IP↔MAC table | `-n` (numeric) | `hostname` or IP → MAC |
| 10 | `nmap host` | Scanning | Port scan | `-sS` (SYN), `-O` (OS), `-p 1-1024` | `PORT`, `STATE`, `SERVICE` |
| 11 | `tcpdump -i eth0` | Capture | Sniff packets | `-w file.pcap`, `-c 100` (count) | Real-time ASCII hex dump |
| 12 | `route -n` | Routing | Routing table | `netstat -rn` (alt) | `Destination`, `Gateway`, `Genmask` |
| 13 | `hostname -I` | Identity | List all IPs | — | `192.168.1.5 10.0.0.2` |
| 14 | `iwconfig` | Wireless | Wi-Fi info | — | `ESSID`, `Frequency`, `Signal` |
| 15 | `whois domain` | DNS | Registration info | — | `Registrar`, `Creation Date` |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick Question:** "What is the difference between `ping localhost` and `ping 127.0.0.1`?" — The answer: **None functionally**; both bypass the network card and loop back inside the kernel. However, `localhost` first requires a **DNS / `/etc/hosts` lookup**, while `127.0.0.1` is a direct numeric call. The packet **never leaves the host** in either case — it is handled by the **loopback virtual interface (`lo`)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Command Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, exam-grade execution flows** for the **most frequently asked commands** in the KTU Lab record and viva. Each walkthrough is structured exactly as a topper's record book would present it.

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — `ifconfig` (Interface Configuration)

### Aim
To display the configuration of all active network interfaces on a Linux host and interpret the fields.

### Command

```bash
ifconfig
```

### Step-by-Step Interpretation

When the command is executed, the system invokes the `net-tools` package's user-space tool, which opens the `/proc/net/dev` and `/proc/net/if_inet6` pseudo-files, then formats the output.

**Sample Output:**

```
eth0      Link encap:Ethernet  HWaddr 00:0c:29:5b:8e:1a
          inet addr:192.168.1.10  Bcast:192.168.1.255  Mask:255.255.255.0
          inet6 addr: fe80::20c:29ff:fe5b:8e1a/64 Scope:Link
          UP BROADCAST RUNNING MULTICAST  MTU:1500  Metric:1
          RX packets:12345 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 frame:0
          TX packets:9876  errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 carrier:0
          collisions:0 txqueuelen:1000
          RX bytes:8543210 (8.1 MiB)  TX bytes:1234567 (1.2 MiB)

lo        Link encap:Local Loopback
          inet addr:127.0.0.1  Mask:255.0.0.0
          inet6 addr: ::1/128 Scope:Host
          UP LOOPBACK RUNNING  MTU:65536  Metric:1
          RX packets:200 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 frame:0
          TX packets:200 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 carrier:0
          collisions:0 txqueuelen:0
          RX bytes:12000 (12.0 KiB)  TX bytes:12000 (12.0 KiB)
```

### Field-by-Field Analytical Matrix

| Field | Value in Example | Meaning | KTU Viva Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Link encap` | Ethernet | Layer-2 encapsulation type | What is "Local Loopback"? |
| `HWaddr` | `00:0c:29:5b:8e:1a` | **MAC address** (48-bit) | Why does it start with `00:0c:29`? (VMware OUI) |
| `inet addr` | `192.168.1.10` | **IPv4 address** assigned to the interface | Private class? → **Class C** |
| `Bcast` | `192.168.1.255` | Broadcast address (last IP in subnet) | Used for ARP & DHCP discover |
| `Mask` | `255.255.255.0` | **Subnet mask** (24 bits set) | Equivalent CIDR → `/24` |
| `MTU` | `1500` | Maximum Transmission Unit in bytes | Why 1500? → IEEE 802.3 standard |
| `RX packets` | `12345` | Total frames **received** (Layer 2) | Includes errors + drops |
| `TX packets` | `9876` | Total frames **transmitted** | Used for throughput calc |
| `RX/TX bytes` | `8.1 MiB / 1.2 MiB` | Total data volume | Divide by 1024² for MiB |
| `lo` interface | `127.0.0.1` | Virtual loopback (always present) | MTU **65536** (special) |

### Alternate Modern Syntax

```bash
# Modern replacement for ifconfig:
ip addr show
ip -s link show eth0     # Adds interface statistics
ip route show            # Routing table
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Writing `ifconfig` syntax in a 2024 KTU exam without mentioning the **`ip`** equivalent will cost marks. Always show **both**.

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — `ping` (Connectivity Test)

### Aim
To verify that a remote host is reachable over the network and measure round-trip time (RTT).

### Command

```bash
ping -c 4 www.google.com
```

### Theoretical Flow (Why ping works)

1. The user issues `ping www.google.com`.
2. The shell invokes the `ping` binary from the `iputils` package.
3. A **DNS resolution** occurs first: `www.google.com` → `142.250.xx.xx` (via `/etc/resolv.conf`).
4. The kernel crafts an **ICMP Echo Request** packet:
   - `Type = 8`, `Code = 0`
   - Identifier = process PID
   - Sequence Number = 0, 1, 2, 3
   - Payload = 56 bytes of `0x00` data
   - **TTL = 64** (Linux default)
5. Packet is encapsulated in **IP → Ethernet** and sent to the gateway.
6. The remote host replies with **ICMP Echo Reply** (Type 0, Code 0).
7. The kernel records the RTT = `T_received − T_sent`.

**Sample Output:**

```
PING www.google.com (142.250.183.68) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 142.250.183.68: icmp_seq=1 ttl=115 time=12.4 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.183.68: icmp_seq=2 ttl=115 time=11.9 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.183.68: icmp_seq=3 ttl=115 time=12.1 ms
64 bytes from 142.250.183.68: icmp_seq=4 ttl=115 time=12.3 ms

--- www.google.com ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% packet loss, time 3004ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 11.900/12.175/12.400/0.190 ms
```

### Analytical Computation (Examiner Favourite)

The **TTL of 115** in the reply tells us the remote host's initial TTL minus the number of hops. Since **Linux defaults to 64** and we observed **115** which is close to **128**, the remote server is likely a **Windows** machine or one with initial TTL = 128, and the number of hops is:

$$
\text{Hops} = \text{Initial\_TTL} - \text{Observed\_TTL}
$$

$$
\text{Hops} = 128 - 115 = 13 \text{ routers}
$$

This is a classic KTU viva question: **"Given ping output showing `ttl=115`, identify the remote OS."**

| Observed TTL in Reply | Likely Remote OS |
|---|---|
| **64** | Linux / macOS / iOS |
| **128** | Windows |
| **255** | Network device (Cisco, Juniper) |

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — `traceroute` (Path Discovery)

### Aim
To identify every router (hop) along the path from source to destination.

### Command

```bash
traceroute www.google.com
```

### The Engineering Behind It (Why it works)

`traceroute` exploits a **clever side-effect of the IPv4 TTL field**:

1. Send a packet with **TTL = 1** to the destination.
2. The first router decrements TTL → 0, drops the packet, and sends back an **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11)** message.
3. `traceroute` records the IP of that router and the RTT.
4. Send a packet with **TTL = 2** → reaches router 2, expires there, reply identifies router 2.
5. Continue with TTL = 3, 4, 5, ... until the destination itself replies with an **ICMP Echo Reply** or **ICMP Port Unreachable** (UDP-probe variant).

**Sample Output:**

```
traceroute to www.google.com (142.250.183.68), 30 hops max
 1  192.168.1.1 (192.168.1.1)        1.234 ms  1.198 ms  1.176 ms
 2  10.10.0.1 (10.10.0.1)            8.456 ms  8.432 ms  8.421 ms
 3  * * *
 4  72.14.215.85 (72.14.215.85)     20.123 ms 20.098 ms 20.087 ms
 ...
14  142.250.183.68 (142.250.183.68)  12.345 ms 12.298 ms 12.276 ms
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `* * *`?** Some routers are configured to **not send ICMP Time Exceeded** (firewall rule). The hop is silent, but traffic still passes through. This is called a **stealth router** or **black-hole** behaviour.

### traceroute vs tracepath

| Feature | `traceroute` | `tracepath` |
|---|---|---|
| Requires root | **Yes** (for ICMP) | **No** |
| Default probe | UDP high ports | ICMP Echo |
| Shows MTU | No | **Yes** (Path MTU Discovery) |
| Output format | Hop + 3 RTTs | Hop + RTT + MTU |

---

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4 — `netstat` & `ss` (Socket Inspection)

### Aim
To list all active TCP/UDP sockets and listening services on the local host.

### Command

```bash
netstat -tulnp
```

### Flag-by-Flag Decoding

| Flag | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-t` | Show **TCP** sockets |
| `-u` | Show **UDP** sockets |
| `-l` | Show only **listening** sockets |
| `-n` | Show numeric addresses (no DNS) |
| `-p` | Show **process ID and name** owning the socket |

**Sample Output:**

```
Proto Recv-Q Send-Q Local Address       Foreign Address     State       PID/Program
tcp        0      0 0.0.0.0:22          0.0.0.0:*           LISTEN      890/sshd
tcp        0      0 127.0.0.1:3306      0.0.0.0:*           LISTEN      1024/mysqld
tcp6       0      0 :::80               :::*                LISTEN      1100/apache2
udp        0      0 192.168.1.10:68     0.0.0.0:*                       750/dhclient
```

### State Machine Interpretation

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `LISTEN` | Server is waiting for incoming connections |
| `ESTABLISHED` | Active 3-way handshake completed |
| `TIME_WAIT` | Connection closed, waiting to clear (2×MSL = 60s) |
| `CLOSE_WAIT` | Remote closed, local hasn't closed yet |
| `SYN_SENT` | Client sent SYN, waiting for SYN-ACK |
| `FIN_WAIT_1` / `FIN_WAIT_2` | Local sent FIN, waiting for ack/remote FIN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Replacement:** Always use `ss -tulnp` in 2024+. It reads directly from the kernel's `tcp_diag` and `udp_diag` modules — **10× faster** than netstat on busy systems.

---

## 3.5 Walkthrough 5 — `nmap` (Network Mapping)

### Aim
To scan a target host for open ports, identify services, and fingerprint the operating system.

### Command

```bash
nmap -sS -p 1-1024 -O 192.168.1.20
```

### Flag-by-Flag Decoding

| Flag | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-sS` | **SYN scan** (half-open / stealth scan) — sends SYN, gets SYN-ACK, sends RST (never completes handshake) |
| `-p 1-1024` | Scan **well-known ports** |
| `-O` | Enable **OS fingerprinting** (sends crafted probes) |
| `-A` | Aggressive: OS + version + script + traceroute |
| `-sV` | Service/version detection |
| `-Pn` | Treat host as online (skip ping) |

**Sample Output:**

```
Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2024-03-15 10:23 IST
Nmap scan report for 192.168.1.20
Host is up (0.0023s latency).
Not shown: 1021 closed ports
PORT     STATE SERVICE     VERSION
22/tcp   open  ssh         OpenSSH 8.2p1
80/tcp   open  http        Apache httpd 2.4.41
443/tcp  open  https       Apache httpd 2.4.41
3306/tcp open  mysql       MySQL 5.7.33
Device type: general purpose
Running: Linux 4.X|5.X
OS details: Linux 4.15 - 5.6
```

### Scan Types Reference (Viva Gold)

| Type | Flag | Packet Sent | Stealth? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TCP Connect** | `-sT` | Full 3-way handshake | ❌ Loud |
| **SYN / Stealth** | `-sS` | SYN → SYN-ACK → RST | ✅ Yes |
| **UDP Scan** | `-sU` | UDP datagram (gets ICMP unreachable) | ⚠ Slow |
| **FIN Scan** | `-sF` | FIN packet | ✅ Bypasses simple filters |
| **NULL Scan** | `-sN` | No flags set | ✅ Bypasses simple filters |
| **Xmas Scan** | `-sX` | FIN + PSH + URG (lit up like a tree) | ✅ Bypasses simple filters |
| **ACK Scan** | `-sA` | ACK only (maps firewall rules) | ✅ |
| **Idle/Zombie Scan** | `-sI` | Uses a third party as zombie | ✅✅ Most stealthy |

> [!WARNING]
> **Legal & Ethical Warning (Viva Q):** Scanning a host **without written permission** is illegal under the **IT Act 2000 (India)** §43 & §66 and equivalent global laws. Only scan your own lab machines.

---

## 3.6 Walkthrough 6 — `tcpdump` (Packet Capture)

### Aim
To capture live network packets on a specified interface and save them for offline analysis.

### Command

```bash
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -c 10 -w capture.pcap
```

### Flag-by-Flag Decoding

| Flag | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `-i eth0` | Listen on interface `eth0` |
| `-c 10` | Capture only **10 packets**, then exit |
| `-w capture.pcap` | Write raw packets to a `.pcap` file (open in Wireshark) |
| `-r capture.pcap` | **Read** a saved pcap |
| `-n` | No DNS resolution (faster) |
| `-v` / `-vv` / `-vvv` | Verbosity (more headers) |
| `host 192.168.1.5` | Filter: only packets to/from that IP |
| `port 80` | Filter: only HTTP traffic |
| `tcp` / `udp` / `icmp` | Filter: by protocol |

### Sample Live Output

```
10:30:01.123456 IP 192.168.1.10.52344 > 142.250.183.68.443: Flags [S], seq 1234567, win 64240, options [mss 1460], length 0
10:30:01.135890 IP 142.250.183.68.443 > 192.168.1.10.52344: Flags [S.], seq 7654321, ack 1234568, win 65535, options [mss 1460], length 0
10:30:01.135920 IP 192.168.1.10.52344 > 142.250.183.68.443: Flags [.], ack 7654322, win 64240, length 0
10:30:01.140123 IP 192.168.1.10.52344 > 142.250.183.68.443: Flags [P.], seq 1234568:1235000, ack 7654322, win 64240, length 432
```

### TCP Flag Reference (Inside tcpdump)

| Symbol | Flag | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `S` | **SYN** | Connection start |
| `.` | **ACK** | Acknowledgement |
| `F` | **FIN** | Graceful close |
| `R` | **RST** | Abrupt reset |
| `P` | **PSH** | Push data to app |
| `U` | **URG** | Urgent pointer |
| `S.` | SYN-ACK | Second step of 3-way handshake |

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Magic:** The output `Flags [S]` then `Flags [S.]` then `Flags [.]` is the famous **TCP 3-Way Handshake**. Spot it in a tcpdump and the examiner's eyes light up.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: How Linux Networking Commands Interact with the Kernel

This Mermaid block diagram shows the **layered communication path** between a user-issued command and the underlying kernel subsystems. **Read it top-down** for execution flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER SHELL: bash / zsh] --> B[USER-SPACE UTILITY]
    B --> C{Command Category}
    C -->|Interface, Routing| D[net-tools / iproute2]
    C -->|ICMP Test| E[iputils - ping / traceroute]
    C -->|Socket Listing| F[net-tools / iproute2 - netstat, ss]
    C -->|DNS Query| G[bind-utils - dig, nslookup, host]
    C -->|Port Scan| H[nmap NSE scripts]
    C -->|Packet Capture| I[tcpdump uses libpcap]
    D --> J[NETLINK SOCKET API]
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> K[RESOLVER LIB - libresolv]
    H --> L[RAW SOCKETS - SOCK_RAW]
    I --> L
    J --> M[KERNEL NETWORK STACK]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[NETFILTER / IPTABLES]
    M --> O[TCP IP LAYER]
    M --> P[NETWORK DEVICE DRIVERS]
    P --> Q[PHYSICAL NIC - eth0, wlan0]
    Q --> R[NETWORK MEDIUM - Cable / WiFi]
```

**Reading guide for viva:**
- `ifconfig`, `ip`, `netstat` → use the **netlink** socket interface (efficient kernel IPC).
- `ping` → uses **ICMP raw sockets** (requires `net.ipv4.ping_group_range`).
- `tcpdump` → uses **libpcap** + **AF_PACKET** raw sockets (requires `CAP_NET_RAW` / root).
- `nmap` for SYN scan → crafts its own raw TCP packets (also needs root).

## 4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake as Observed by tcpdump

This sequence diagram shows the **exact flag transitions** that `tcpdump` will display during a `curl https://example.com` call.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT 192.168.1.10
    participant S as SERVER 93.184.216.34
    Note over C,S: Three-Way Handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK)
    C->>S: 1 SYN seq=1000 win=64240 [Flags S]
    S-->>C: 2 SYN-ACK seq=5000 ack=1001 win=65535 [Flags S.]
    C->>S: 3 ACK ack=5001 win=64240 [Flags .]
    Note over C,S: Data Transfer (PSH-ACK)
    C->>S: 4 HTTP GET request [Flags P.]
    S-->>C: 5 HTTP 200 OK + HTML body [Flags P.]
    Note over C,S: Graceful Close (FIN, ACK, FIN-ACK)
    C->>S: 6 FIN seq=1100 [Flags F.]
    S-->>C: 7 FIN-ACK seq=5500 ack=1101 [Flags F.]
    C->>S: 8 ACK ack=5501 [Flags .]
    Note over C,S: Connection fully closed
```

## 4.3 Command Selection Flowchart (When to use what?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WHAT DO YOU WANT TO DO?] --> B{Choose Goal}
    B -->|See my IP / MAC| C[ip addr show]
    B -->|Test if a host is alive| D[ping -c 4 host]
    B -->|Find the path to a host| E[traceroute host]
    B -->|See open services on a host| F[nmap host]
    B -->|See my open ports| G[ss -tulnp]
    B -->|Resolve a domain name| H[dig hostname]
    B -->|Capture live traffic| I[tcpdump -i eth0]
    B -->|See the LAN neighbours| J[arp -a]
    B -->|See my routing table| K[ip route show]
    C --> L[Document in Record Book]
    D --> L
    E --> L
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagrams are schematic representations.** In the KTU record, you may also include **screenshots of the actual terminal output** and a hand-drawn **network topology** showing the lab LAN with the host, gateway, and the server you pinged.

---
