<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Continuous Evaluation (CE) — Record book + viva: **40 marks**
> - End Semester Exam (ESE) — Practical exam + oral: **60 marks**
> - **Internal choice is mandatory** in ESE Part B (i.e., write EITHER A OR B, not both).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `ping` and `traceroute` commands in Linux. What protocols do they use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `ping` | `traceroute` |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Test if a remote host is reachable | Discover the *path* (routers) to the host |
| **Protocol Used** | **ICMP Echo Request (Type 8) / Echo Reply (Type 0)** | **UDP high ports** (default in Linux) → expects **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11)** at each hop, and **ICMP Port Unreachable** at destination |
| **Key Mechanism** | Single packet, single reply | Sends packets with **incrementing TTL** (1, 2, 3, …) to map each hop |
| **Output** | RTT (min/avg/max), packet loss | Hop number, three RTTs, IP of each router |
| **Root Required** | No | Yes (for UDP/ICMP raw) — `tracepath` is the non-root alternative |

**(1 mark for protocol, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for differentiating table/points.)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between `netstat` and `ss`? Why is `ss` recommended in modern Linux distributions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Functionally** both list sockets: `netstat -tulnp` ≈ `ss -tulnp`. *(1 mark)*
2. **Architecture:** `netstat` reads from the **slow `/proc` filesystem** by parsing `/proc/net/tcp`, `/proc/net/udp`. `ss` reads directly from the kernel via the **netlink** `tcp_diag`/`udp_diag` modules, making it **10–100× faster** on systems with thousands of sockets. *(1 mark)*
3. **Why `ss` is recommended:** `net-tools` (containing `netstat`, `ifconfig`, `route`) was **officially deprecated** in 2019 and is no longer installed by default in RHEL 8+, Ubuntu 20+, Debian 11+. Modern scripts use the **`iproute2`** suite which provides `ss`, `ip`, and `tc`. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS TO STUDENT (ESE Pattern):** *Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) **Explain the `ifconfig` command in detail. List all the fields displayed in its output and state their significance. Compare it with the modern `ip addr` command.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Purpose of `ifconfig` (1 mark):**
`ifconfig` (*interface configurator*) is a legacy Unix command from the `net-tools` package used to **view and configure** network interface parameters: IP address, subnet mask, broadcast address, MAC address, MTU, and packet statistics. It is being phased out in favour of the `iproute2` suite.

**2. Field-by-Field Output Interpretation (4 marks):**

Consider the output:
```
eth0  Link encap:Ethernet  HWaddr 00:0c:29:5b:8e:1a
      inet addr:192.168.1.10  Bcast:192.168.1.255  Mask:255.255.255.0
      UP BROADCAST RUNNING MULTICAST  MTU:1500  Metric:1
      RX packets:12345 errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 frame:0
      TX packets:9876  errors:0 dropped:0 overruns:0 carrier:0
      collisions:0 txqueuelen:1000
      RX bytes:8543210 (8.1 MiB)  TX bytes:1234567 (1.2 MiB)
```

| Field | Significance |
|---|---|
| `Link encap:Ethernet` | Layer-2 framing type (Ethernet II, IEEE 802.3) |
| `HWaddr` | 48-bit hardware (MAC) address, e.g., `00:0c:29:5b:8e:1a` |
| `inet addr` | IPv4 address assigned (192.168.1.10) |
| `Bcast` | Broadcast address for the subnet (192.168.1.255) |
| `Mask` | Subnet mask (255.255.255.0 = /24) |
| `UP` | Interface is administratively enabled |
| `BROADCAST` | Supports broadcast |
| `RUNNING` | Cable connected / carrier present |
| `MULTICAST` | Supports multicast (IGMP) |
| `MTU:1500` | Maximum Transmission Unit = 1500 bytes (Ethernet default) |
| `Metric:1` | Routing cost (used by older routing daemons) |
| `RX packets/bytes` | Cumulative received frames & bytes since boot |
| `TX packets/bytes` | Cumulative transmitted frames & bytes |
| `errors`, `dropped`, `overruns`, `frame`, `carrier`, `collisions` | Error counters — used to diagnose link health |

**3. `ifconfig` vs `ip addr` (2 marks):**

| Aspect | `ifconfig` | `ip addr` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `net-tools` (deprecated) | `iproute2` (current) |
| Interface to kernel | `ioctl()` system calls | **Netlink** sockets (modern) |
| CIDR support | No (uses dotted mask) | **Yes** — `192.168.1.10/24` |
| Can show multiple addresses? | No (one IP per line) | **Yes** — lists all secondary IPs |
| Setting an IP | `ifconfig eth0 10.0.0.1` | `ip addr add 10.0.0.1/24 dev eth0` |
| Default in 2024 distros? | ❌ | ✅ |

---

#### (b) **Demonstrate the use of `traceroute` command with a suitable example. Explain how it works internally and identify the protocol messages exchanged at each stage.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Demonstration (2 marks):**

```bash
$ traceroute www.iitb.ac.in
traceroute to www.iitb.ac.in (103.21.244.5), 30 hops max, 60 byte packets
 1  192.168.1.1 (192.168.1.1)        1.234 ms  1.198 ms  1.176 ms
 2  10.10.0.1 (10.10.0.1)            8.456 ms  8.432 ms  8.421 ms
 3  * * *
 4  72.14.215.85 (72.14.215.85)     20.123 ms 20.098 ms 20.087 ms
 ...
14  103.21.244.5 (103.21.244.5)     45.234 ms 45.198 ms 45.176 ms
```

**2. Internal Working (3 marks):**

`traceroute` exploits the **TTL (Time-To-Live)** field in the IPv4 header. The mechanism is:

- It sends a series of packets (UDP by default, with target port starting at 33434) addressed to the destination.
- **Packet 1** has TTL = 1. The first router decrements TTL to 0, **drops the packet**, and sends back an **ICMP Type 11 (Time Exceeded)** message to the source.
- `traceroute` records the IP and RTT of that router.
- **Packet 2** has TTL = 2 → reaches router 2, gets dropped, ICMP Type 11 returned.
- This continues, incrementing TTL by 1, until the **packet reaches the destination** itself.
- The destination host, not listening on UDP port 33434, replies with **ICMP Type 3, Code 3 (Port Unreachable)** — which `traceroute` interprets as "destination reached."

**3. Protocol Exchange Summary (2 marks):**

| Step | Source | Destination | Probe | Reply | Field Decoded |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hop 1 | Host | R1 | UDP TTL=1 | ICMP Type 11 | R1's IP, RTT |
| Hop 2 | Host | R1→R2 | UDP TTL=2 | ICMP Type 11 from R2 | R2's IP, RTT |
| … | … | … | TTL=k | ICMP Type 11 from Rk | Rk's IP, RTT |
| Final | Host | Dest | UDP TTL=N | ICMP Type 3 Code 3 | Dest's IP, RTT |

> [!TIP]
> **Variants:** `traceroute -I` uses ICMP Echo (mimics Windows). `traceroute -T` uses TCP SYN to port 80 (bypasses ICMP-blocked firewalls).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) **With suitable examples, explain the following: (i) `nmap` SYN scan, (ii) `tcpdump` packet capture. How are raw sockets involved in these operations?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) `nmap` SYN Scan (3 marks):**

The SYN scan (`nmap -sS <host>`) is called a **"half-open" or "stealth" scan** because the TCP three-way handshake is never completed. The sequence is:

1. `nmap` sends a **TCP packet with only the SYN flag** set to the target port.
2. If the port is **open**, the target replies with **SYN + ACK**.
3. `nmap` immediately sends a **RST** to tear down the connection (no ACK is sent, so the handshake is half-completed).
4. If the port is **closed**, the target replies with **RST + ACK**.
5. If the port is **filtered** (firewall), no reply is received → timeout.

**Example output:**
```
$ nmap -sS 192.168.1.20
PORT    STATE  SERVICE
22/tcp  open   ssh
80/tcp  open   http
443/tcp open   https
```

**(ii) `tcpdump` Packet Capture (2 marks):**

`tcpdump` puts the NIC into **promiscuous mode** and copies every frame that arrives. The frames are passed up to user-space via **AF_PACKET** sockets. Example:

```bash
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -c 5 -nn
```
Output shows timestamps, source/destination IP, port, TCP flags, and sequence numbers in ASCII form. The `-w` flag saves raw frames to a `.pcap` file for Wireshark analysis.

**Raw Socket Involvement (2 marks):**

Both `nmap` and `tcpdump` require **CAP_NET_RAW** capability (root privileges on Linux) because:
- Standard sockets (`SOCK_STREAM`, `SOCK_DGRAM`) are tied to a specific 4-tuple and a kernel-managed connection state.
- Crafting custom packets (SYN without ACK, custom TTL, spoofed source) requires **SOCK_RAW** which bypasses kernel state machines.
- Linux has the **`ping_group_range`** sysctl and the **`net.ipv4.ip_forward`** switch governing raw-socket access.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students write that "tcpdump modifies the packets" — **WRONG**. It only *observes* (copies) them. The original packet is delivered to the OS stack unchanged.

---

#### (b) **A system administrator notices that a critical web server at IP `192.168.10.50` is unreachable from the network. As a network engineer, describe the **step-by-step troubleshooting procedure** using the Linux networking commands you have learned. State the expected output at each stage.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

This is a classic **layered-troubleshooting** question, the OSI-down approach:

| Step | Command | Purpose | Expected Healthy Output | Failure Indication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ping 127.0.0.1` | Verify **local TCP/IP stack** is functional | `64 bytes from 127.0.0.1: time<1ms` | If fails → local stack broken (reinstall drivers) |
| 2 | `ip addr show` (or `ifconfig`) | Verify **own IP is assigned** | `inet 192.168.10.x/24` `state UP` | No IP → DHCP issue or interface down |
| 3 | `ping 192.168.10.1` (default gateway) | Verify **local LAN & gateway** | Replies with `ttl=64` | If fails → switch / cable / driver fault |
| 4 | `ping 8.8.8.8` | Verify **Internet (L3) reachability** | Replies with `ttl` value | If fails → gateway / NAT / firewall issue |
| 5 | `ping www.google.com` | Verify **DNS resolution** | Replies with IP | If fails → DNS misconfigured (check `/etc/resolv.conf`) |
| 6 | `traceroute 192.168.10.50` | Identify **exact hop where path breaks** | List of routers with RTTs | `* * *` at hop *k* → router *k* is dropping packets |
| 7 | `arp -a` (on same LAN) | Verify **ARP resolution** works | MAC of 192.168.10.50 listed | "incomplete" → ARP not resolving → L2 fault |
| 8 | `nmap -p 80 192.168.10.50` | Verify **port 80 is open** on target | `80/tcp open http` | `closed` or `filtered` → service down / firewall |
| 9 | `tcpdump -i eth0 host 192.168.10.50` | **Live capture** to see if SYN leaves & reply returns | SYN from us, SYN-ACK from server | Only SYN out, no SYN-ACK → server or firewall blocking |
| 10 | `ss -tulnp` | Verify **local service** is actually listening | `LISTEN 0.0.0.0:80` | Nothing on port 80 → web server not started |

**Conclusion Logic (1 mark):**
- If steps 1–3 succeed but 4 fails → **routing/NAT** problem at gateway.
- If 1–4 succeed but 5 fails → **DNS** problem.
- If 1–5 succeed but 6 fails at hop *k* → **router *k* or its firewall** is the culprit.
- If 1–8 succeed but 9 shows no return traffic → **destination host firewall**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - ❌ Skipping step 1 (loopback test) — loses 1 mark because you cannot isolate *where* the fault lies.
> - ❌ Saying "ping the server" without first verifying your own IP — you cannot ping if your interface is down.
> - ❌ Jumping to `nmap` without checking if the IP is even reachable. `nmap` does host-discovery but it cannot *guarantee* what the lower layers are doing.
> - ✅ **Always** present the troubleshooting in **OSI bottom-up order** to score full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print this!)**

- ✅ **`ifconfig`** shows IP, MAC, MTU, RX/TX statistics — **deprecated**, use `ip addr`.
- ✅ **`ping`** uses **ICMP Echo (Type 8)**; reply is **ICMP Echo Reply (Type 0)**.
- ✅ **TTL in ping reply** tells OS: **64 → Linux**, **128 → Windows**, **255 → Cisco/Juniper**.
- ✅ **`traceroute`** sends packets with **incrementing TTL**; routers reply with **ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11)**.
- ✅ **`tracepath`** is the **non-root** alternative to traceroute; also discovers MTU.
- ✅ **`netstat -tulnp`** → TCP, UDP, Listening, numeric, with PID. Modern replacement is **`ss`**.
- ✅ **`ss`** reads from kernel via `tcp_diag` — **much faster** than netstat.
- ✅ **`nslookup`** and **`dig`** are DNS tools; `dig` is the modern, verbose choice.
- ✅ **`arp -a`** displays the **IP ↔ MAC** table on the local LAN.
- ✅ **`nmap -sS`** is the **SYN stealth scan** — completes 2 of 3 handshake steps, never sends the final ACK.
- ✅ **`nmap` scan types** (memorise the table): `-sT`, `-sS`, `-sU`, `-sF`, `-sN`, `-sX`, `-sA`, `-sI`.
- ✅ **`tcpdump`** uses **libpcap** + **AF_PACKET raw sockets**; requires `sudo`/root.
- ✅ **TCP flag symbols in tcpdump:** `S`=SYN, `.`=ACK, `F`=FIN, `R`=RST, `P`=PSH, `U`=URG.
- ✅ **Three-Way Handshake in tcpdump:** `S` → `S.` → `.`
- ✅ **Loopback address `127.0.0.1`** never leaves the host; MTU on `lo` is **65536**.
- ✅ **MTU of Ethernet is 1500 bytes** (IEEE 802.3 standard).
- ✅ **Port classes:** 0–1023 (privileged), 1024–49151 (registered), 49152–65535 (ephemeral).
- ✅ **Tools deprecated since 2019:** `ifconfig`, `netstat`, `route`, `arp` (in many distros). Modern equivalents: `ip`, `ss`, `ip route`, `ip neigh`.
- ✅ **Legal note:** Scanning a host without written permission is illegal under the **IT Act 2000** (India).
- ✅ **Order of TCP states observed during connection:** `LISTEN → SYN_SENT → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT → CLOSED`.
- ✅ **ICMP message types you must remember:** 0 (Echo Reply), 3 (Dest Unreachable), 8 (Echo Request), 11 (Time Exceeded).
- ✅ **The mnemonic "I P T N N T"** — *Interface, Ping, Trace, Netstat, Nmap, Tcpdump* — covers the entire experiment.

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Confidence Hack:** When asked *"What is the protocol used by ping?"*, do not stop at "ICMP". Say: *"ICMP Type 8 (Echo Request) sent, expecting ICMP Type 0 (Echo Reply). The source IP of the reply is the target's IP, the destination IP is our own, and the RTT is computed in the kernel as `now − send_timestamp`. The TTL we observe in the reply, after decrementing at each hop, can be used to fingerprint the remote OS."* — That is **full marks** every time.

---

*End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCSL507 Networks Lab)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HTTP packet capture and analysis using Wireshark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL507 - NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** HTTP packet capture and analysis using Wireshark ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is an application-layer request-response protocol defined in **RFC 9110 (2022)**, operating as the foundational data communication standard of the World Wide Web. In the KTU 2024 PCCSL507 Networks Lab context, HTTP packet capture refers to the systematic interception, logging, and inspection of HTTP request and response messages exchanged between a **web client (browser)** and a **web server** across the underlying TCP/IP stack, using a protocol analyzer. **Wireshark** is an open-source, cross-platform **packet sniffer and network protocol analyzer** (previously known as Ethereal). It places the host machine's NIC into **promiscuous mode** (or uses loopback capture for local traffic) to capture every frame traversing the interface, decoding each layer of the **TCP/IP encapsulation model** for human-readable inspection. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PCCSL507 – Module 2):** This experiment is mapped to **CO2: "Analyze the working of network protocols using packet capture tools"** and demonstrates the practical operation of the **Application Layer (HTTP)** riding over the **Transport Layer (TCP)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sending a **registered post parcel** to a friend through India Post: - The **HTTP Request** is the **letter inside the parcel** (your actual demand — "send me the KTU syllabus page"). - The **TCP Header** is the **courier slip** pinned outside (tracking number, source/destination addresses, order of delivery). - The **IP Header** is the **address on the warehouse bag** (your PIN code, the post office routing). - The **Ethernet Frame** is the **delivery truck** that physically carries the bag through the road. - **Wireshark** is a **security camera mounted at the sorting hub** that photographs the outside of every parcel, reads the courier slip, and even X-rays to show what's inside — all without opening anything itself. In one sentence: **Wireshark lets you "listen in" on every conversation your computer has with the internet, frame by frame.** ### 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters > [!NOTE] > - **HTTP is unencrypted by default** (plaintext on port **80**), which is why beginners can read every character — making it the perfect teaching protocol. > - **HTTPS (port 443)** would show only encrypted gibberish; hence KTU chooses **HTTP** specifically for this lab. > - Capturing local loopback traffic (`127.0.0.1` or `localhost`) requires **Npcap loopback adapter** on Windows or raw `lo` interface on Linux. ### 1.4 Key Constants and Metrics to Remember | Metric | Standard Value | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | Default HTTP Port | **TCP Port 80** | Differentiated from HTTPS (443) | | HTTP Method (GET) | 3 bytes (ASCII) | Smallest practical request | | Ethernet MTU | **1500 bytes** | Max payload per frame | | Wireshark Timestamp Resolution | **microsecond (μs)** | Used for round-trip calculations | | Promiscuous Mode | Bit set on NIC | Required for non-broadcast capture | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The OSI / TCP-IP Layering of an HTTP Transaction When a browser fetches `http://example.com/index.html`, the data traverses a strict 5-layer encapsulation cascade. Wireshark displays this in a **reverse (top-down) view** for the analyst: | Layer # | Protocol (Wireshark Column) | Function in HTTP Context | |---|---|---| | 5 – Application | **HTTP** | Carries the request line, headers, body | | 4 – Transport | **TCP** | Guarantees ordered, reliable byte stream via port 80 | | 3 – Network | **IP** | Global addressing & routing between client and server | | 2 – Data Link | **Ethernet II** | LAN-level MAC addressing & frame check | | 1 – Physical | – | Electrical/optical signalling (not visible to Wireshark) | ### 2.2 Anatomy of an HTTP Request A single HTTP GET request contains the following logical blocks: 1. **Request Line** — `Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version CRLF` Example: `GET /index.html HTTP/1.1\r\n` 2. **Header Fields** — Zero or more `Field-Name: Field-Value\r\n` lines (Host, User-Agent, Accept, Accept-Language, Connection, etc.) 3. **Empty Line** — A single `CRLF` (`\r\n`) acting as the **header-body separator** 4. **Message Body** — Optional (empty for GET, present for POST) ### 2.3 Anatomy of an HTTP Response 1. **Status Line** — `HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase CRLF` Example: `HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n` 2. **Header Fields** — Server, Content-Type, Content-Length, Date, Last-Modified, Connection, etc. 3. **Empty Line** — `CRLF` separator 4. **Message Body** — The actual HTML/JSON/image bytes requested ### 2.4 The Hidden Pre-Step: TCP 3-Way Handshake HTTP **never** opens its own connection — it always rides on a pre-established TCP session. Therefore, before any HTTP packet appears in Wireshark, you will see three SYN packets: 1. **SYN** — Client → Server (Seq=x) 2. **SYN-ACK** — Server → Client (Seq=y, Ack=x+1) 3. **ACK** — Client → Server (Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1) > [!IMPORTANT] > The total time from SYN to the final ACK is the **TCP Connection Setup Latency**. In KTU viva voce, you may be asked to identify the three flags — look for the **SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK** packets preceding the first `[HTTP]` row. ### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet The following relationships are used to compute performance metrics directly from Wireshark's `Time` column. $$ \text{Round-Trip Time (RTT)} = T_{\text{ACK\_received}} - T_{\text{SYN\_sent}} $$ $$ \text{Time to First Byte (TTFB)} = T_{\text{HTTP\_Response\_first\_byte}} - T_{\text{HTTP\_Request\_sent}} $$ $$ \text{Total Page Load Time} = T_{\text{last\_packet}} - T_{\text{first\_SYN}} $$ $$ \text{Throughput (bytes/sec)} = \frac{\sum (\text{Frame Length})}{T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}} $$ $$ \text{Average Packet Size} = \frac{\sum \text{Frame Length in bytes}}{N_{\text{packets}}} $$ | Quantity | Wireshark Column to Read | Unit | |---|---|---| | Inter-arrival time | `Time delta from previous displayed` | seconds | | Cumulative time | `Time` (relative to first packet) | seconds | | Payload size | `Length` column | bytes | | TCP sequence | `Sequence number` field | bytes (32-bit) | | Window size | `Window size value` | bytes | | TTL (hop limit) | `Time to live` | hops (max **64** for Linux, **128** for Windows) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Network engineers at companies like Akamai and Cloudflare use the exact same metrics to debug slow website loading, optimize TCP window scaling, and detect HTTP request floods (Layer-7 DDoS). ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Standard Experimental Procedure (as expected in KTU Record) The following sequence is the **prescribed step-by-step procedure** for this lab experiment: 1. **Launch Wireshark** → Select the active interface (Wi-Fi / Ethernet) and start capture. 2. **Open a browser** → Type `http://example.com` (or any plaintext HTTP site like `http://info.cern.ch`). 3. **Press Enter** → Wait for page to load fully. 4. **Stop capture** in Wireshark. 5. **Apply Display Filter** → type `http` in the filter bar and press Enter. 6. **Identify** the `GET / HTTP/1.1` request and the corresponding `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` response. 7. **Expand** the `Hypertext Transfer Protocol` and `Transmission Control Protocol` sections to read each field. 8. **Record** the values into the observation table. ### 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating Round-Trip Time Suppose Wireshark displays the following three packets at the start of the capture: | Packet # | Protocol | Info | Time (s) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | TCP | 52344 → 80 [SYN] | 0.000000 | | 2 | TCP | 80 → 52344 [SYN, ACK] | 0.038412 | | 3 | TCP | 52344 → 80 [ACK] | 0.038820 | | 4 | HTTP | GET / HTTP/1.1 | 0.039105 | | 5 | HTTP | HTTP/1.1 200 OK (text/html) | 0.127664 | **Step 1: Compute TCP Handshake RTT** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTT}_{\text{handshake}} &= T_{\text{SYN-ACK}} - T_{\text{SYN}} \\ &= 0.038412 - 0.000000 \\ &= \mathbf{38.412 \, ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Server Response Latency (TTFB)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TTFB} &= T_{\text{first\_response\_byte}} - T_{\text{request\_sent}} \\ &= 0.127664 - 0.039105 \\ &= \mathbf{88.559 \, ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Total Page Load Time** (assume last packet at 0.412308 s) $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{last}} - T_{\text{first\_SYN}} \\ &= 0.412308 - 0.000000 \\ &= \mathbf{412.308 \, ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute Throughput** Suppose the entire HTTP body plus headers sums to **32,000 bytes** delivered between t = 0.039105 s and t = 0.412308 s. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Duration} &= 0.412308 - 0.039105 = 0.373203 \, \text{s} \\ \text{Throughput} &= \frac{32{,}000 \, \text{bytes}}{0.373203 \, \text{s}} \\ &= 85{,}746.84 \, \text{bytes/s} \\ &\approx \mathbf{83.7 \, KB/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Convert to bits per second (bps) for network benchmarking** $$ 85{,}746.84 \times 8 = 685{,}974.7 \, \text{bps} \approx \mathbf{670 \, kbps} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Example: Decoding the HTTP Request Line Wireshark displays the following bytes in the HTTP section: ``` 0000 47 45 54 20 2f 20 48 54 54 50 2f 31 2e 31 0d 0a ``` **Step-by-step ASCII decoding:** | Hex | ASCII | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 47 45 54 | `GET` | HTTP Method | | 20 | ` ` | Space | | 2f | `/` | Request URI (root) | | 20 | ` ` | Space | | 48 54 54 50 2f 31 2e 31 | `HTTP/1.1` | Protocol version | | 0d 0a | `\r\n` | End of request line (CRLF) | This reproduces the line: **`GET / HTTP/1.1`** — the canonical request for any webpage. ### 3.4 Worked Example: Status Code Classification | Wireshark Status Line | Code Class | KTU Viva Interpretation | |---|---|---| | `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` | **2xx Success** | Resource retrieved successfully | | `HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently` | **3xx Redirection** | Browser must follow to new URL | | `HTTP/1.1 404 Not Found` | **4xx Client Error** | Wrong URL typed | | `HTTP/1.1 500 Internal Server Error` | **5xx Server Error** | Server-side code failure | > [!IMPORTANT] > The 3-digit code's first digit tells the class (2/3/4/5); the second and third are the specific reason. A KTU favourite viva question is: *"What does 304 Not Modified mean?"* — Answer: the cached copy is still valid, no body sent. ### 3.5 Worked Example: Estimating Hop Count from TTL If the captured HTTP response shows `Time to live = 56` and the server is known to use Linux (default starting TTL = 64): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Hops traversed} &= \text{Initial TTL} - \text{Observed TTL} \\ &= 64 - 56 \\ &= \mathbf{8 \, hops} \end{aligned} $$ If the server runs Windows (default TTL = 128), the same packet would imply: $$ 128 - 56 = \mathbf{72 \, hops} \quad \text{(unrealistic — confirms Linux OS)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Operating System Fingerprinting via TTL is a classic passive technique** taught alongside this experiment in advanced network forensics modules. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete HTTP Transaction Lifecycle in Wireshark ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Client Browser Opens
http://example.com"] --> B["DNS Query
UDP Port 53"] B --> C["DNS Response
IP Address Resolved"] C --> D["TCP SYN
Seq=x"] D --> E["TCP SYN-ACK
Seq=y, Ack=x+1"] E --> F["TCP ACK
Connection Established"] F --> G["HTTP GET Request
GET / HTTP/1.1"] G --> H["TCP ACK
Server Acknowledges Request"] H --> I["HTTP 200 OK Response
text/html Body"] I --> J["TCP ACK
Client Acknowledges Response"] J --> K["TCP FIN
Connection Tear-down"] K --> L["Wireshark Display
Filter: http"] L --> M["Analyst Reads
Time, Length, Flags"] style A fill:#cfe2ff style G fill:#fff3cd style I fill:#d1e7dd style L fill:#f8d7da style M fill:#e2e3e5 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Wireshark Three-Pane Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Top Pane: Packet List
(Summary of all captured frames)"] --> B["Middle Pane: Packet Details
(Expandable protocol tree)"] B --> C["Bottom Pane: Packet Bytes
(Raw hex + ASCII dump)"] A2["Columns: No, Time, Source, Destination, Protocol, Length, Info"] -.-> A B2["Frame > Ethernet > IP > TCP > HTTP
Click + to expand each layer"] -.-> B C2["Offset 0x00 to 0xFF
Left=Hex, Right=ASCII"] -.-> C style A fill:#cfe2ff style B fill:#d1e7dd style C fill:#fff3cd ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Essential Wireshark Display Filters for HTTP Lab ```mermaid flowchart TD F1["Filter: http"] --> F1a["Shows only HTTP request/response"] F2["Filter: tcp.port == 80"] --> F2a["Shows full TCP stream on port 80"] F3["Filter: ip.addr == 192.168.1.10"] --> F3a["Filters by specific host IP"] F4["Filter: http.request.method == GET"] --> F4a["Shows only GET requests"] F5["Filter: http.response.code == 200"] --> F5a["Shows only successful responses"] F6["Right-click HTTP packet > Follow > HTTP Stream"] --> F6a["Reconstructs full conversation"] style F1 fill:#cfe2ff style F4 fill:#d1e7dd style F6 fill:#f8d7da ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: TCP Header Fields Wireshark Exposes ```mermaid flowchart TD H["TCP Header (20-60 bytes)"] --> H1["Source Port (16 bits)"] H --> H2["Destination Port (16 bits)"] H --> H3["Sequence Number (32 bits)"] H --> H4["Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)"] H --> H5["Flags: SYN, ACK, FIN, RST, PSH, URG"] H --> H6["Window Size (16 bits)"] H --> H7["Checksum (16 bits)"] H --> H8["Urgent Pointer (16 bits)"] H5 --> H5a["SYN = Connection Start"] H5 --> H5b["ACK = Data Acknowledged"] H5 --> H5c["FIN = Connection Close"] H5 --> H5d["RST = Abrupt Reset"] style H fill:#fff3cd style H5 fill:#cfe2ff style H5a fill:#d1e7dd style H5b fill:#d1e7dd style H5c fill:#d1e7dd style H5d fill:#f8d7da ``` > [!NOTE] > These diagrams together give a complete **functional architecture** of the Wireshark workflow — the three-pane UI, the filter language, the TCP header anatomy, and the end-to-end HTTP transaction — which together form the conceptual map for the viva voce. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** *What is Wireshark? Mention any two uses of it in network analysis.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Wireshark is a free, open-source **packet sniffer and protocol analyzer** used to capture and inspect data packets traversing a network interface in real time. (1 mark) Two uses: 1. **Troubleshooting** — Diagnosing slow networks, packet loss, or failed TCP handshakes. (1 mark) 2. **Protocol analysis** — Decoding and verifying HTTP, DNS, TCP, IP headers for academic learning and security audits. (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *List the three packets exchanged during a TCP 3-way handshake that precedes an HTTP request.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **SYN** — Client sends synchronization packet with initial sequence number to the server. (1 mark) 2. **SYN-ACK** — Server acknowledges the client's SYN and sends its own SYN. (1 mark) 3. **ACK** — Client acknowledges the server's SYN; the connection is now established and HTTP can begin. (1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B Question Choice 1 (14 Marks) > **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]* **(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to capture and analyze an HTTP packet using Wireshark. List the observations to be recorded. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the TCP 3-way handshake that occurs before an HTTP transaction. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] 1. Open Wireshark → select the active network interface (e.g., `eth0` or `Wi-Fi`). (1 mark) 2. Click the **Start Capture** button (blue shark-fin icon). (1 mark) 3. Open a web browser and visit any plaintext HTTP site, e.g., `http://example.com`. (1 mark) 4. Wait for the page to load completely. (0.5 marks) 5. Return to Wireshark and click **Stop Capture** (red square icon). (0.5 marks) 6. In the display-filter bar, type `http` and press Enter. (1 mark) 7. Identify the `GET / HTTP/1.1` request and the corresponding `HTTP/1.1 200 OK` response. (0.5 marks) 8. Click a packet → expand the `Hypertext Transfer Protocol` layer in the middle pane. (0.5 marks) 9. Record observations: **Source IP, Destination IP, Source Port, Destination Port, Method, URI, Status Code, Content-Type, Frame Length, Time delta**. (1 mark) #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **TCP 3-Way Handshake Diagram (textual schematic):** ``` Client Server |---- SYN (Seq=x) ------------------>| [Step 1] |<--- SYN, ACK (Seq=y, Ack=x+1) -----| [Step 2] |---- ACK (Seq=x+1, Ack=y+1) ------->| [Step 3] | ==== Connection Established ==== | |---- HTTP GET / HTTP/1.1 ---------->| [Step 4] |<--- HTTP/1.1 200 OK ------------- | [Step 5] ``` **Explanation (1 mark per correct point):** 1. **SYN** — The client initiates the connection by sending a TCP segment with the **SYN flag set** and an initial sequence number `x`. (1 mark) 2. **SYN-ACK** — The server responds with both the **SYN and ACK flags set**, acknowledging `x+1` and providing its own sequence number `y`. (1 mark) 3. **ACK** — The client sends a final **ACK** confirming `y+1`, completing the handshake. (1 mark) 4. After this, both sides are synchronized and HTTP can begin. (1 mark) 5. Wireshark identifies these packets by their `Info` column showing `[SYN]`, `[SYN, ACK]`, `[ACK]`. (1 mark) 6. The first HTTP packet (`GET / HTTP/1.1`) appears **only after** the third ACK. (1 mark) 7. The total duration of this handshake is the **TCP setup latency** and is the first metric a network engineer measures. (1 mark) --- ### 5.3 Part B Question Choice 2 (14 Marks) > **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]* **(a)** Describe the structure of an HTTP request and response message with neat labelled diagrams. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Wireshark capture shows the SYN packet at time 0.000000 s, the SYN-ACK at 0.045120 s, and the first HTTP response byte at 0.187450 s. Compute (i) the TCP handshake RTT, (ii) the Time to First Byte (TTFB), and (iii) the total page load time if the last packet is observed at 0.512300 s. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **HTTP Request Structure (ASCII diagram, 3.5 marks):** ``` +---------------------------------------------------+ | GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 \r\n <-- Request Line| | Host: www.example.com\r\n | | User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 ...\r\n | | Accept: text/html\r\n | | Accept-Language: en-US\r\n | | Connection: keep-alive\r\n | | \r\n <-- Empty Line | | (empty for GET) | +---------------------------------------------------+ ``` **HTTP Response Structure (ASCII diagram, 3.5 marks):** ``` +---------------------------------------------------+ | HTTP/1.1 200 OK \r\n <-- Status Line | | Server: Apache/2.4.41\r\n | | Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8\r\n | | Content-Length: 1234\r\n | | Date: Mon, 15 Jul 2024 12:00:00 GMT\r\n | | Connection: keep-alive\r\n | | \r\n <-- Empty Line | | ... <-- Message Body | +---------------------------------------------------+ ``` **Key points (1 mark distributed across explanations):** - The **Request Line** and **Status Line** are the only mandatory single lines. (0.25 mark) - Headers are key-value pairs separated by `: ` and terminated by CRLF. (0.25 mark) - The empty line is mandatory — it separates headers from body. (0.25 mark) - GET requests typically have **no body**; POST does. (0.25 mark) #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given data:** - $T_{\text{SYN}} = 0.000000$ s - $T_{\text{SYN-ACK}} = 0.045120$ s - $T_{\text{HTTP\_response\_first\_byte}} = 0.187450$ s - $T_{\text{last\_packet}} = 0.512300$ s - We need $T_{\text{HTTP\_request\_sent}}$; assume the GET was sent immediately after the final ACK at $T_{\text{SYN-ACK}} + \text{negligible} = 0.045120$ s (standard assumption for KTU). **Step 1: TCP Handshake RTT** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{RTT}_{\text{handshake}} &= T_{\text{SYN-ACK}} - T_{\text{SYN}} \\ &= 0.045120 - 0.000000 \\ &= \mathbf{0.045120 \, s} = \mathbf{45.12 \, ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Time to First Byte (TTFB)** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TTFB} &= T_{\text{first\_response\_byte}} - T_{\text{request\_sent}} \\ &= 0.187450 - 0.045120 \\ &= \mathbf{0.142330 \, s} = \mathbf{142.33 \, ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Total Page Load Time** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{total}} &= T_{\text{last}} - T_{\text{first\_SYN}} \\ &= 0.512300 - 0.000000 \\ &= \mathbf{0.512300 \, s} = \mathbf{512.30 \, ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Inferences** (1 mark) - The handshake took **45.12 ms** — typical for a near-CDN server. - The TTFB (**142.33 ms**) is the dominant delay — server processing time. - A healthy page load is **under 1 second**; this 512.30 ms is **acceptable** for a small static page. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students LOSE marks in this experiment:** > 1. **Confusing Wireshark's "Time" column with absolute wall-clock time.** It is *relative* to the first captured packet. (–1 mark typical) > 2. **Forgetting to count the TCP 3-way handshake as part of the transaction.** Examiners expect you to mention it explicitly. (–2 marks) > 3. **Writing "port 8080" or "port 443" as the default HTTP port.** It is **port 80**. (–1 mark) > 4. **Mixing up HTTP methods** — GET retrieves, POST submits, PUT updates, DELETE removes. (–1 mark) > 5. **Not mentioning `Content-Type` and `Content-Length` headers** in the response — these are mandatory fields the examiner specifically checks. (–1 mark) > 6. **Capturing HTTPS traffic and writing "I could not see the data"** — the experiment explicitly requires an **HTTP** site. Always use `http://example.com`, never `https://`. (–2 marks) > 7. **Missing the empty line (`\r\n`)** between headers and body in the diagram — examiners are strict about this separator. (–1 mark) > 8. **Not stating the operating system fingerprinting** logic via TTL — high-yield viva topic. (–1 mark) --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **HTTP** is plaintext, request-response, application-layer; **default port 80**; rides on **TCP**. - **Wireshark** captures traffic in **promiscuous mode** and decodes the **5-layer TCP/IP stack** in three panes. - A typical HTTP transaction in Wireshark = **3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) + GET request + 200 OK response + FIN teardown**. - **Key Wireshark columns:** No, Time, Source, Destination, Protocol, Length, Info. - **Key filters:** `http`, `tcp.port == 80`, `http.request.method == GET`, `ip.addr == x.x.x.x`. - **Right-click → Follow → TCP Stream** reconstructs the full HTTP conversation in ASCII. - **RTT** = $T_{\text{SYN-ACK}} - T_{\text{SYN}}$; **TTFB** = $T_{\text{first\_response}} - T_{\text{request\_sent}}$; **Total Load Time** = $T_{\text{last}} - T_{\text{first\_SYN}}$. - **Status code classes:** 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error. - **TTL-based OS fingerprinting:** Linux initial TTL = 64; Windows = 128; subtract observed TTL to estimate hops. - **MTU** of Ethernet is **1500 bytes**; HTTP headers typically 200–800 bytes; body size from `Content-Length`. - **Always use HTTP (not HTTPS)** in this lab to see plaintext data. - **For local captures**, use `lo` interface on Linux or **Npcap loopback adapter** on Windows. - **Examiners love:** diagrams of HTTP request/response, explanation of TCP handshake, and computation of RTT/TTFB from given timestamps.

topics.title: SMTP packet capture and analysis using Wireshark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCSL507 - NETWORKS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** SMTP packet capture and analysis using Wireshark ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol)** is an application-layer protocol defined in **RFC 5321** that operates over **TCP port 25** (default) and is used for the reliable, connection-oriented transmission of electronic mail (email) between mail servers and from a client (Mail User Agent) to a mail server (Mail Submission Agent). **Wireshark** is an open-source network protocol analyzer (packet sniffer) that captures live network traffic from a physical/wireless interface and allows the user to inspect each protocol layer (Ethernet → IP → TCP → Application) of the captured packets in a structured, color-coded dissector pane. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCSL507)** > The lab experiment aims to demonstrate the **text-based, command-response nature** of SMTP, the underlying **TCP 3-way handshake**, and the **multi-stage mail transaction** (HELO/EHLO → MAIL → RCPT → DATA → QUIT) by manually generating SMTP traffic and observing it live in Wireshark. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office Letter" Model Imagine SMTP as the **rules used by a post office clerk when you hand over a letter**: | Real World | SMTP Equivalent | |---|---| | You walk up to the post office counter | **TCP connection** is established (3-way handshake) | | The clerk asks, "Who are you?" | Server sends **220 Service Ready** | | You say "Hello, I'm Mr. Amal" | Client sends **HELO/EHLO** with its domain | | You say, "This letter is FROM me" | Client sends **MAIL FROM:<>** | | You say, "Send it TO Alice" | Client sends **RCPT TO:** | | You dictate the letter body | Client sends **DATA** command followed by message | | The clerk says, "Message accepted for delivery" | Server sends **250 OK** | | You leave the counter | Client sends **QUIT** (TCP FIN teardown) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Intuition:** SMTP is a *push* protocol — it only **delivers mail to a server's storage area (mailbox)**. It does **not retrieve** mail from the server. Retrieval is the job of **POP3 (port 110)** or **IMAP (port 143)**. In the KTU lab, you typically use `smtp.gmail.com` on port **587** (submission) or run a local server like **hMailServer / Postfix** on port **25** to avoid authentication encryption issues. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Ports (KTU-Memorizable) | Metric | Value | Note | |---|---|---| | **Default SMTP Port** | **25** | Server-to-server relay (MTA → MTA) | | **Submission Port** | **587** | Client (MUA) → MSA, with STARTTLS | | **Legacy Secure Port** | **465** | Implicit SMTPS (deprecated) | | **Transport Protocol** | **TCP** | Connection-oriented, reliable | | **Packet Size (typical)** | **< 1500 bytes** (MTU) | Fits in a single Ethernet frame | | **Encoding** | **7-bit ASCII** | NVT (Network Virtual Terminal) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 SMTP Architecture & Transaction Phases SMTP follows a strict **client-server, request-response** model where the client (sending mail server/MUA) issues commands and the server (receiving mail server/MDA) replies with a **3-digit status code** followed by human-readable text. Every SMTP session consists of **four logical phases**: 1. **Connection Establishment Phase** - TCP 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) on port 25. - Server immediately transmits `220 Service ready`. 2. **Mail Transaction Phase (Envelop)** - `HELO` (RFC 821) or `EHLO` (RFC 1425, Extended SMTP) — client identification. - `MAIL FROM:` — declares the sender. - `RCPT TO:` — declares one recipient (can be repeated). - `DATA` — triggers message body transmission, terminated by `.`. 3. **Optional Authentication Phase** - `AUTH LOGIN` or `AUTH PLAIN` — sends base64-encoded credentials. - Used on submission port **587** to prevent open relay abuse. 4. **Connection Termination Phase** - `QUIT` — client requests session close. - Server replies `221 Service closing transmission channel`. - TCP issues FIN-ACK-FIN-ACK (4-way teardown). ### 2.2 SMTP Command Vocabulary (KTU Cheat Sheet) | Command | Argument | Purpose | Expected Server Code | |---|---|---|---| | `HELO` | Client domain | Identify client (basic SMTP) | 250 | | `EHLO` | Client domain | Identify client (Extended SMTP) | 250 (multi-line) | | `MAIL FROM` | `` | Reverse-path of mail | 250 | | `RCPT TO` | `` | Forward-path of mail | 250 / 550 | | `DATA` | — | Begin message body | 354 → 250 | | `RSET` | — | Abort current transaction | 250 | | `VRFY` | Username | Verify mailbox existence | 250 / 252 / 550 | | `NOOP` | — | No-operation (keepalive) | 250 | | `QUIT` | — | End session | 221 | | `AUTH` | Mechanism | Authenticate client | 235 / 334 / 535 | ### 2.3 SMTP Response Code Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield) | Class | Meaning | Examples (Code: Meaning) | |---|---|---| | **2xx** | **Positive Completion** | **220**: Service ready / **250**: OK, request completed / **221**: Service closing / **235**: Auth successful | | **3xx** | **Positive Intermediate** | **334**: Server challenge (AUTH) / **354**: Start mail input, end with `.` | | **4xx** | **Transient Negative** (retry) | **421**: Service not available / **450**: Mailbox busy | | **5xx** | **Permanent Negative** (no retry) | **500**: Syntax error / **550**: Mailbox unavailable / **535**: Auth failed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multi-line responses** (used by `EHLO`) use the hyphen separator: `250-SIZE 10240000` (continues) and `250 HELP` (final line). Wireshark displays the full multi-line response as a single SMTP packet. ### 2.4 Wireshark Capture Filters & Display Filters (Exam Must-Know) | Type | Filter Syntax | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Capture Filter** | `port 25` | Capture only SMTP traffic at capture time | | **Capture Filter** | `host and tcp port 25` | Narrow to specific client-server pair | | **Display Filter** | `smtp` | Show all SMTP packets | | **Display Filter** | `smtp.req.command` | Show only client requests | | **Display Filter** | `smtp.resp.code == 250` | Show only "OK" responses | | **Display Filter** | `tcp.port == 587` | Show submission port traffic | | **Display Filter** | `smtp.data.fragment` | Show DATA payload fragments | | **Follow Stream** | Right-click → `Follow → TCP Stream` | Reconstruct entire SMTP dialogue | > [!NOTE] > **Anti-Hallucination Note:** Wireshark filter keywords are case-insensitive and use dot notation for protocol fields (e.g., `smtp.resp.code`, `tcp.flags.syn`). The actual displayed field name may vary slightly by Wireshark version (v3.x vs v4.x). ### 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Cybersecurity & Forensics:** SMTP traffic analysis detects **spam relays, phishing, and data exfiltration** via email attachments. - **Mail Server Administration:** Diagnoses **delivery failures, blacklisting, and STARTTLS misconfigurations**. - **Network Engineering:** Validates **firewall rules, port 25 blocking (common in ISPs to curb spam)**, and load balancer behavior. - **Academic / KTU Lab:** Demonstrates the **OSI model layer encapsulation** (Application → Transport → Network → Data Link) using a real, interactive, plain-text protocol. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: A Complete SMTP Session (Telnet to Local SMTP Server) Assume a KTU student opens `cmd`, types `telnet 127.0.0.1 25`, and the local **hMailServer** is listening. Below is the **raw transaction** that Wireshark will capture, broken step-by-step with expected packet structure. **Step 1 — TCP 3-Way Handshake (Captured as TCP Packets)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} &: \quad \text{SYN, Seq = x, Ack = 0} \\ \text{Server} \rightarrow \text{Client} &: \quad \text{SYN-ACK, Seq = y, Ack = x+1} \\ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} &: \quad \text{ACK, Seq = x+1, Ack = y+1} \end{aligned} $$ > In Wireshark, these appear in the **Protocol column** as `TCP` with `[SYN]`, `[SYN, ACK]`, `[ACK]` flags set. **Source Port** = ephemeral (e.g., 49152), **Destination Port** = `25`. **Step 2 — Server Greeting (Server → Client)** $$ \text{220} \;\; \text{mail.ktulab.local} \;\; \text{ESMTP} \;\; \text{Service Ready} $$ Wireshark dissection: `SMTP Response: 220` in the bottom pane; `smtp.resp.code == 220`; payload visible in ASCII. **Step 3 — Client Identification (Client → Server)** $$ \text{EHLO} \;\; \text{client.k tulab.local} $$ Server reply (multi-line Extended SMTP): $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{250-mail.ktulab.local Hello [127.0.0.1]} \\ &\text{250-SIZE 10485760} \\ &\text{250-PIPELINING} \\ &\text{250-STARTTLS} \\ &\text{250-8BITMIME} \\ &\text{250 SMTPUTF8} \end{aligned} $$ > Wireshark shows this as a **single SMTP packet** with `smtp.response.code: 250` and a multi-line argument listing server capabilities. **Step 4 — Mail Transaction Start** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} &: \quad \text{MAIL FROM:} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{250 OK} \\ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} &: \quad \text{RCPT TO:} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{250 OK} \\ \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} &: \quad \text{DATA} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{354 Start mail input; end with .} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Message Body Transmission (DATA payload)** The client transmits the RFC 5322 formatted message: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{From: amal@ktulab.local} \\ &\text{To: bob@ktulab.local} \\ &\text{Subject: KTU Networks Lab Test} \\ &\text{Date: Mon, 15 Sep 2025 10:30:00 +0530} \\ & \\ &\text{This is a test SMTP message captured in Wireshark.} \\ &.\\ \end{aligned} $$ The terminal line containing **only a period (`.`)** terminates the DATA block. Server replies `250 OK: Message queued`. **Step 6 — Connection Termination** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server} &: \quad \text{QUIT} \quad \rightarrow \quad \text{221 Service closing transmission channel} \\ \text{TCP Teardown} &: \quad \text{FIN-ACK-FIN-ACK (4-way)} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Decoding a Single SMTP Packet in Wireshark The following table maps the **Wireshark packet detail panes** to the **OSI layers** a student must identify for a 14-mark KTU question: | Wireshark Pane / Field | OSI Layer | Protocol Field | Example Value (from above session) | |---|---|---|---| | Frame | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Frame Number, Length | 12, 74 bytes | | Ethernet II | Layer 2 | Src/Dst MAC | `00:0C:29:...` | | Internet Protocol v4 | Layer 3 | Src/Dst IP, TTL, Protocol | `127.0.0.1`, TTL=64, Proto=TCP (6) | | Transmission Control Protocol | Layer 4 | Src/Dst Port, Seq, Ack, Flags | Src=`49152`, Dst=`25`, Flags=`PSH, ACK` | | Simple Mail Transfer Protocol | Layer 7 (Application) | Command, Argument, Response Code | `Command: MAIL` / `Response: 250` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wireshark Color Coding (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva):** > - **Light Green** = TCP handshake/SACK > - **Light Blue** = UDP > - **Pale Yellow / Lavender** = TCP data (SMTP/HTTP/FTP) > - **Black with red text** = Packets with errors (e.g., retransmissions, malformed SMTP) > - **Gray** = Packets not dissected (encrypted SMTP/STARTTLS) ### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Why a Single Email May Produce Multiple Packets $$ \begin{aligned} N_{packets} &= N_{handshake} + N_{cmd\_resp} + N_{data\_payloads} + N_{teardown} \\ N_{handshake} &= 3 \quad (\text{SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK}) \\ N_{cmd\_resp} &= 2k \quad \text{(command + response for each of } k \text{ exchanges)} \\ N_{data\_payloads} &= \left\lceil \frac{\text{Message\_Size}}{\text{TCP\_MSS}} \right\rceil \quad \text{where MSS} = 1460 \text{ bytes typically} \\ N_{teardown} &= 4 \quad (\text{FIN-ACK, ACK, FIN-ACK, ACK}) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Example:** For a 5 KB email body: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{data\_payloads} &= \left\lceil \frac{5120}{1460} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.507 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ segments} \\ \Rightarrow \text{Total packets} &\approx 3 + (2 \times 6) + 4 + 4 = 23 \text{ packets (approximate)} \end{aligned} $$ Where the 6 command-response pairs include: greeting (1) + EHLO (1) + MAIL (1) + RCPT (1) + DATA-ack (1) + DATA-OK (1) + QUIT-OK (1) ≈ 7 pairs depending on STARTTLS. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Complete SMTP Session Flow (State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> TCP_Handshake TCP_Handshake --> ServerGreeting : SYN SYNACK ACK ServerGreeting --> ClientHelo : 220 Service Ready ClientHelo --> ServerEhloReply : EHLO client.domain ServerEhloReply --> MailFrom : 250 OK multiline MailFrom --> ServerMailOk : MAIL FROM:sender ServerMailOk --> RcptTo : 250 OK RcptTo --> ServerRcptOk : RCPT TO:receiver ServerRcptOk --> DataCmd : 250 OK DataCmd --> ServerDataReady : DATA ServerDataReady --> MessageBody : 354 Start input MessageBody --> ServerDataOk : CRLF dot CRLF ServerDataOk --> QuitCmd : 250 Queued QuitCmd --> ServerBye : QUIT ServerBye --> TCPTeardown : 221 Bye TCPTeardown --> [*] : FIN ACK FIN ACK ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SMTP Capture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client MUA
Telnet or Mail Client] -->|TCP SYN
Dst Port 25| B[Server MTA
Port 25 Listening] B -->|SYN ACK| A A -->|ACK| B B -->|220 Service Ready| A A -->|EHLO domain| B B -->|250 multiline caps| A A -->|MAIL FROM| B B -->|250 OK| A A -->|RCPT TO| B B -->|250 OK| A A -->|DATA| B B -->|354 Start input| A A -->|Message body dot| B B -->|250 Queued| A A -->|QUIT| B B -->|221 Closing| A A -->|FIN| B B -->|FIN ACK| A W[Wireshark Engine
Live Capture] -.->|Packet Buffer| D[pcap File
kcap] W --> F1[Capture Filter
port 25] W --> F2[Display Filter
smtp] D --> V1[Packet List Pane] D --> V2[Packet Details Pane
OSI Layer View] D --> V3[Packet Bytes Pane
Hex and ASCII] D --> V4[Follow TCP Stream] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Lab Procedure Workflow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step1: Install and Start
hMailServer or Postfix] --> P2[Step2: Configure Wireshark
Capture Interface] P2 --> P3[Step3: Apply Capture Filter
port 25 or host IP] P3 --> P4[Step4: Open Telnet
telnet 127.0.0.1 25] P4 --> P5[Step5: Send SMTP Commands
EHLO MAIL RCPT DATA QUIT] P5 --> P6[Step6: Stop Capture
Save as pcap] P6 --> P7[Step7: Analyze Packets
Identify 3 way handshake] P7 --> P8[Step8: Follow TCP Stream
Reconstruct Dialogue] P8 --> P9[Step9: Map Layers
Ethernet IP TCP SMTP] P9 --> P10[Step10: Document and
Submit Lab Record] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: SMTP Packet Encapsulation (OSI Stack View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L7 [Layer 7 Application] X1[SMTP Payload
EHLO client domain CRLF] end subgraph L4 [Layer 4 Transport] X2[TCP Header
SrcPort DstPort25 Seq Ack Flags PSH ACK] end subgraph L3 [Layer 3 Network] X3[IP Header
SrcIP DstIP TTL64 Protocol6] end subgraph L2 [Layer 2 Data Link] X4[Ethernet II Header
SrcMAC DstMAC EtherType 0800] end subgraph L1 [Layer 1 Physical] X5[Bits on Wire
Preamble Frame Check Sequence] end L7 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP PCCSL507]`** **Define SMTP. List any four SMTP commands with their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol), defined in **RFC 5321**, is a text-based, application-layer protocol used for **transmitting electronic mail** between mail servers and clients. It operates over **TCP port 25** using a command-response dialogue. - **Four Commands (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **HELO / EHLO** — Client identifies itself to the server. 2. **MAIL FROM** — Specifies the sender's email address (reverse-path). 3. **RCPT TO** — Specifies one recipient's address (forward-path), repeatable. 4. **DATA** — Indicates the start of the message body; terminated by `.`. 5. *(Any valid 4th: QUIT, RSET, NOOP, VRFY, AUTH)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model QP PCCSL507]`** **Explain the significance of SMTP response codes 220, 250, 354, and 550.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Code | Class | Meaning (½ mark each) | |---|---|---| | **220** | 2xx | Service ready — server greeting sent immediately after TCP connection. | | **250** | 2xx | OK / Requested mail action completed successfully (used after MAIL, RCPT, DATA, QUIT). | | **354** | 3xx | Start mail input — server invites client to send DATA payload; end with `.`. | | **550** | 5xx | Mailbox unavailable / permanent failure — recipient does not exist or relay is denied. | > [!NOTE] > Examiner's Tip: Students must specify the **class** (2xx/3xx/5xx) along with the code to earn the full 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **complete SMTP session phases** between a client and server. List all commands exchanged and the response codes expected. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Phases of SMTP Session:** **Phase 1 – Connection Establishment (1 mark):** The client opens a TCP connection to the server on **port 25**. The 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) is performed, after which the server immediately transmits: $$ 220 \;\; \text{mail.example.com} \;\; \text{ESMTP} \;\; \text{Service Ready} $$ **Phase 2 – Client Identification (1 mark):** The client sends either `HELO` (basic SMTP) or `EHLO` (Extended SMTP, recommended) with its domain name. The server replies with a multi-line `250 OK` listing supported extensions (SIZE, STARTTLS, 8BITMIME, etc.). **Phase 3 – Mail Transaction (3 marks):** The client issues three commands in sequence: $$ \begin{aligned} &\texttt{MAIL FROM:} \quad \rightarrow \quad 250 \text{ OK} \\ &\texttt{RCPT TO:} \quad \rightarrow \quad 250 \text{ OK} \\ &\texttt{DATA} \quad \rightarrow \quad 354 \text{ Start mail input; end with .} \end{aligned} $$ After `DATA`, the client sends headers (`From:`, `To:`, `Subject:`, `Date:`), a blank line, and the message body, terminated by a line containing only a period. The server replies `250 OK: Message queued for delivery`. **Phase 4 – Connection Termination (1 mark):** The client sends `QUIT`; the server replies `221 Service closing transmission channel`. The TCP connection is then closed via the 4-way teardown (FIN-ACK-FIN-ACK). **Diagram (1 mark) — see Mermaid Diagram 1 in Section 4.** --- **(b)** Explain how Wireshark is used to **capture and analyze** SMTP traffic. List the **display filters** used to isolate SMTP commands and responses, and describe the **Follow TCP Stream** feature. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **1. Setting Up the Capture (2 marks):** - Launch Wireshark, select the live interface (e.g., `Ethernet` or `Loopback` for `127.0.0.1`). - Optionally apply a **capture filter** like `port 25` or `host 192.168.1.10 and tcp port 25` to limit recording. - Click **Start** to begin packet capture. **2. Generating SMTP Traffic (1 mark):** - Open `cmd` and run `telnet 127.0.0.1 25` (or use a mail client like Thunderbird with SMTP configured). - Execute the SMTP dialogue: `EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, message body, `QUIT`. **3. Stopping and Saving (1 mark):** - Click **Stop** in Wireshark, then **File → Save As → .pcap** for record submission. **4. Analysis & Display Filters (2 marks):** | Display Filter | Function | |---|---| | `smtp` | Show all SMTP packets | | `smtp.req.command` | Show only client requests (HELO, MAIL, RCPT, DATA, QUIT) | | `smtp.resp.code == 250` | Show only "OK" responses | | `tcp.port == 25` | Show all traffic on SMTP port | | `smtp.response.code == 354` | Locate the start of DATA body | **5. Follow TCP Stream (1 mark):** Right-click any SMTP packet → **Follow → TCP Stream** reconstructs the **entire client-server dialogue as a single ASCII transcript** in a pop-up window (e.g., blue text for client, red for server). This is the most efficient way to **show the complete mail transaction** in a viva or lab record. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`** **(a)** Explain the **OSI layer encapsulation of an SMTP packet** captured in Wireshark. Show how each layer contributes its own header and identify the protocol fields at every layer. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** When a client sends `MAIL FROM:`, Wireshark captures a frame that traverses all **5 relevant OSI layers**. Each layer prepends its own header to the payload received from the layer above — this is **encapsulation**. **1. Layer 7 – Application (SMTP) (2 marks):** - **Protocol:** Simple Mail Transfer Protocol - **Data:** `MAIL FROM:\r\n` - **Wireshark field:** `Simple Mail Transfer Protocol → Request: MAIL, Argument: ` **2. Layer 4 – Transport (TCP) (2 marks):** - **Source Port:** 49152 (ephemeral) | **Destination Port:** 25 - **Sequence Number:** 0 (or relative) | **Acknowledgment Number:** Server Seq + 1 - **Flags:** `PSH, ACK` (Push the data immediately + acknowledge) - **Window Size:** 65535 (typical) - **Checksum:** 0xABCD (auto-validated by Wireshark) - **Wireshark field:** `Transmission Control Protocol → Src Port, Dst Port, Seq, Ack, Flags` **3. Layer 3 – Network (IPv4) (1 mark):** - **Source IP:** 192.168.1.10 | **Destination IP:** 192.168.1.5 - **TTL:** 64 | **Protocol:** 6 (TCP) - **Total Length:** 79 bytes - **Wireshark field:** `Internet Protocol Version 4 → Src, Dst, TTL, Protocol` **4. Layer 2 – Data Link (Ethernet II) (1 mark):** - **Source MAC:** 00:0C:29:AB:CD:EF - **Destination MAC:** 00:0C:29:12:34:56 - **EtherType:** 0x0800 (IPv4) - **Wireshark field:** `Ethernet II → Src, Dst, Type` **5. Layer 1 – Physical (1 mark):** - The assembled frame is converted to **bits** and transmitted as electrical/optical signals on the medium. Wireshark does not directly display Layer 1, but the **frame length and timestamp** imply the physical transmission. **Encapsulation Summary (in equation form):** $$ \text{Frame} = \text{Ethernet Header} + \text{IP Header} + \text{TCP Header} + \text{SMTP Payload} + \text{FCS} $$ --- **(b)** Differentiate between **SMTP, POP3, and IMAP** protocols. Explain why SMTP is used with port **25**, **587**, and **465** for different scenarios. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **1. Protocol Comparison (4 marks):** | Feature | **SMTP** (RFC 5321) | **POP3** (RFC 1939) | **IMAP** (RFC 3501) | |---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | **Send / push** mail to server | **Retrieve / pull** mail from server | **Retrieve and manage** mail on server | | **Default Port** | **25** | **110** | **143** | | **Direction** | Client → Server (Upload) | Client ← Server (Download) | Client ↔ Server (Sync) | | **Mail Storage** | Server stores after delivery | Typically **downloads and deletes** from server | Mail **stays on server** (multi-device) | | **Commands** | HELO, MAIL, RCPT, DATA, QUIT | USER, PASS, RETR, DELE, QUIT | LOGIN, SELECT, FETCH, STORE, SEARCH | | **Offline Access** | N/A | Yes (after download) | Requires internet (sync model) | | **Folder Support** | None | No | Yes (INBOX, Sent, Drafts) | **2. SMTP Port Variants (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Port 25} \;&:\; \text{Server-to-Server (MTA}\rightarrow\text{MTA) relay, often blocked by ISPs to prevent spam.} \\ \text{Port 587} \;&:\; \text{Message SUBMISSION (MUA}\rightarrow\text{MSA), requires STARTTLS, RFC 6409 standard.} \\ \text{Port 465} \;&:\; \text{Implicit SMTPS over TLS (deprecated but still used by some legacy clients).} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Loss Areas):** > 1. **Confusing SMTP with POP3/IMAP** — SMTP is for *sending*, not receiving. Many students write "SMTP retrieves mail," losing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the TCP 3-way handshake** — When asked to explain SMTP, you must mention that it runs over **TCP** and begins with a handshake. Marks are lost for treating SMTP as a connectionless protocol. > 3. **Ignoring the `DATA` terminator** — Many students write that `DATA` ends the message but forget the `.` (period on a line by itself). > 4. **Port confusion** — Writing "SMTP uses port 110" is a guaranteed **−1 mark** deduction. > 5. **No diagram** — Part B (a) sub-questions in KTU ESE *expect* a diagram (Mermaid/block diagram) of the SMTP flow. Skipping it costs 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Bullets — KTU 2024 PCCSL507 Module 2** - ☐ **SMTP** = **Simple Mail Transfer Protocol**, RFC 5321, Application layer, **TCP-based**, **text-based** (ASCII/NVT). - ☐ **Default port = 25**, Submission = **587**, Legacy SSL = **465** (deprecated). - ☐ SMTP is a **push** protocol (sending only). **POP3/IMAP** are for receiving. - ☐ Every SMTP session = **TCP 3-way handshake → Server 220 → EHLO/HELO → MAIL FROM → RCPT TO → DATA → body → QUIT → 4-way teardown**. - ☐ DATA body terminates with a **single period `.`** on its own line. - ☐ Response codes: **2xx = success**, **3xx = intermediate**, **4xx = transient retry**, **5xx = permanent failure**. - ☐ Common codes: **220** (ready), **250** (OK), **354** (start input), **535** (auth failed), **550** (mailbox unavailable), **221** (closing). - ☐ `EHLO` triggers **multi-line** `250-` responses listing ESMTP extensions (SIZE, STARTTLS, 8BITMIME). - ☐ **Wireshark capture filter:** `port 25` | **Display filter:** `smtp`, `smtp.req.command`, `smtp.resp.code == 250`. - ☐ **Follow TCP Stream** reconstructs the **entire ASCII dialogue** — single most useful Wireshark feature for this lab. - ☐ SMTP packet = **Ethernet (L2) + IPv4 (L3) + TCP (L4) + SMTP (L7)** — **5-layer encapsulation** must be identified in Wireshark's detail pane. - ☐ **Wireshark color:** Light Green = TCP control (SYN/FIN), Pale Lavender = TCP data carrying SMTP payload. - ☐ A single email yields **≈ 20–25 packets** (3 handshake + ~14 command-response + 3–5 data segments + 4 teardown). - ☐ Lab tools commonly used: **hMailServer / Postfix / MailEnable** (server) + **Telnet / Thunderbird / smtplib** (client) + **Wireshark** (analyzer). - ☐ **Always save the .pcap file** and include a screenshot of the **Follow TCP Stream** in your lab record — these are mandatory submission items for KTU 2024 Scheme PCCSL507.

topics.title: DNS packet capture and analysis using Wireshark
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL507 - NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** DNS packet capture and analysis using Wireshark

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is DNS?

**Domain Name System (DNS)** is the hierarchical, distributed naming system of the internet that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `103.25.61.5`). It is formally defined in **RFC 1034** (Concepts and Facilities) and **RFC 1035** (Implementation and Specification).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *DNS is an application-layer protocol that operates over UDP port **53** (primarily) and TCP port **53** (for large responses and zone transfers). It belongs to the **C/S (Client–Server)** model and uses a distributed database architecture.*

## 1.2 What is Wireshark?

**Wireshark** is the world's foremost open-source **network protocol analyzer**. It captures live packets from a network interface in **promiscuous mode** and presents them with granular protocol-level dissection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU's PCCSL507 Networks Lab, Wireshark is the **mandatory tool** for all packet capture and analysis experiments. Default capture filter port: **53** for DNS.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Internet's Phonebook & The Microscope

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Technical Role |
|---|---|---|
| DNS Resolver | A receptionist looking up a phone number | Recursive lookup server (e.g., `8.8.8.8`) |
| Authoritative DNS Server | The actual phonebook holder | Returns final A/AAAA records |
| Domain Name | Person's name (`John`) | Easy-to-remember hostname |
| IP Address | Phone number (`+91-471-2544000`) | Routable network address |
| Wireshark | A **digital microscope** | Captures and dissects packets on the wire |
| DNS Packet | A conversation between two people | A UDP/TCP datagram with structured TLV fields |

> [!TIP]
> **Why capture DNS packets?** To diagnose slow resolutions, verify query–response timing, detect **DNS spoofing / cache poisoning**, and learn how the application layer is actually encoded on the wire (OSI Layer 7 → 4 → 2 → 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DNS Architecture — The Resolved Hierarchy

DNS is organized as an **inverted tree**:

```
                       .  (Root — 13 logical root servers: a.root-servers.net ... m.root-servers.net)
                       |
        -----------------------------------
        |               |               |
      .com             .in            .org          (TLD Servers)
        |               |
    ----------      -----------
    |        |      |         |
 google.com  yahoo  ktu    iitb              (Authoritative Servers)
    |
www.google.com → 142.250.193.68            (Resource Records)
```

- **Root Server (.)** — Top of the hierarchy, knows about all TLDs.
- **TLD Server (.com, .in, .org)** — Knows about second-level domains.
- **Authoritative Server** — Holds the final A/AAAA/MX/CNAME records.
- **Recursive Resolver** — Performs the lookup on behalf of the client (e.g., ISP resolver, `8.8.8.8`).

## 2.2 Two Modes of Resolution

1. **Recursive Query** — Client asks resolver; resolver is *fully responsible* for returning the final answer.
2. **Iterative Query** — Resolver queries each server in turn; each server returns a *referral* (not the answer), except the authoritative one.

## 2.3 DNS Header Format (RFC 1035) — 12 Bytes Fixed

The DNS header is a precise bit-packed structure. Each field has a fixed bit-width:

| Offset | Field | Width (bits) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Transaction ID (ID) | 16 | Matches query ↔ response |
| 2 | Flags | 16 | QR, Opcode, AA, TC, RD, RA, Z, AD, CD, RCODE |
| 4 | QDCOUNT | 16 | Number of questions |
| 6 | ANCOUNT | 16 | Number of answer RRs |
| 8 | NSCOUNT | 16 | Number of authority RRs |
| 10 | ARCOUNT | 16 | Number of additional RRs |

### Flags Field Decomposition (16 bits)

| Bit(s) | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | QR | 0 = query, 1 = response |
| 1–4 | OPCODE | 0 = standard query, 1 = inverse, 2 = status |
| 5 | AA | Authoritative Answer (response only) |
| 6 | TC | Truncation flag |
| 7 | RD | Recursion Desired |
| 8 | RA | Recursion Available |
| 9–11 | Z | Reserved (must be 0) |
| 12 | AD | Authenticated Data (DNSSEC) |
| 13 | CD | Checking Disabled (DNSSEC) |
| 14–15 | RCODE | 0 = no error, 3 = NXDOMAIN, 2 = SERVFAIL |

## 2.4 DNS Message — Question & Resource Record Format

### Question Section
| Field | Size |
|---|---|
| QNAME | Variable (label-length pairs, max 63 per label, 255 total) |
| QTYPE | 16 bits |
| QCLASS | 16 bits (IN = 1) |

### Resource Record (RR) Section — Used in Answer/Authority/Additional
| Field | Size | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| NAME | Variable | Often a pointer (compression) |
| TYPE | 16 bits | A=1, NS=2, CNAME=5, MX=15, AAAA=28, SOA=6, PTR=12 |
| CLASS | 16 bits | IN=1 |
| TTL | 32 bits | Cache lifetime in seconds |
| RDLENGTH | 16 bits | Length of RDATA |
| RDATA | Variable | Type-specific payload |

## 2.5 Common DNS Record Types — KTU High-Yield Table

| Type | Code | Purpose | RDATA Format |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | IPv4 address | 4 bytes |
| AAAA | 28 | IPv6 address | 16 bytes |
| CNAME | 5 | Canonical name alias | Domain name |
| MX | 15 | Mail exchanger | Preference (16) + domain |
| NS | 2 | Name server | Domain name |
| PTR | 12 | Reverse lookup | Domain name |
| SOA | 6 | Start of Authority | MNAME, RNAME, serial, refresh, retry, expire, minimum |
| TXT | 16 | Text records | Arbitrary text |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — DNS Header Byte Math

| Quantity | Formula | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Header size | $H_{DNS} = 12$ bytes | **12 bytes** |
| Fixed part of Question | $Q_{fix} = QTYPE + QCLASS$ | **4 bytes** |
| IPv4 A record RDATA | $R_{A} = 4$ bytes | **4 bytes** |
| IPv6 AAAA record RDATA | $R_{AAAA} = 16$ bytes | **16 bytes** |
| Total DNS message size (typical) | $H_{DNS} + QNAME + Q_{fix} + \sum RR_i$ | $\leq 512$ bytes (UDP) |
| Encoded label length | $L_i = 1 + \text{len}(\text{label}_i)$ | varies |
| Message compression pointer | $P = 0xC0 \lor \text{offset}$ | 2 bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> DNS over UDP is **limited to 512 bytes**. If the response exceeds this, the **TC (Truncation)** bit is set, and the client retries over **TCP** — observed directly in Wireshark as a `DNS` query with `transport: TCP`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of DNS Header Bit Layout (The Flags Byte)

The 16-bit **Flags** field is the most-tested KTU concept. We derive its packing:

$$
\text{Flags} = \underbrace{QR}_{1} \cdot 2^{15} \;+\; \underbrace{OPCODE}_{4} \cdot 2^{11} \;+\; \underbrace{AA}_{1} \cdot 2^{10} \;+\; \underbrace{TC}_{1} \cdot 2^{9} \;+\; \underbrace{RD}_{1} \cdot 2^{8} \;+\; \underbrace{RA}_{1} \cdot 2^{7} \;+\; \underbrace{Z}_{3} \cdot 2^{4} \;+\; \underbrace{AD}_{1} \cdot 2^{3} \;+\; \underbrace{CD}_{1} \cdot 2^{2} \;+\; \underbrace{RCODE}_{4} \cdot 2^{0}
$$

### Worked Example 1 — Decode a Standard Response Flags Value

Suppose Wireshark shows the Flags field as hex `0x8180`. Decode it.

$$
\text{Flags}_{\text{hex}} = 0x8180 = 0x8000 \;+\; 0x0180
$$

**Step 1 — Extract QR bit (bit 15):**

$$
QR = \left\lfloor \frac{0x8180 \; \& \; 0x8000}{0x8000} \right\rfloor = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{This is a RESPONSE}
$$

**Step 2 — Extract Opcode (bits 11–14):**

$$
OPCODE = \frac{0x8180 \; \& \; 0x7800}{0x0800} = \frac{0x0000}{0x0800} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Standard Query}
$$

**Step 3 — Extract AA (bit 10), TC (bit 9), RD (bit 8), RA (bit 7):**

$$
AA = \frac{0x8180 \; \& \; 0x0400}{0x0400} = 0
$$

$$
TC = \frac{0x8180 \; \& \; 0x0200}{0x0200} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Not truncated}
$$

$$
RD = \frac{0x8180 \; \& \; 0x0100}{0x0100} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Recursion Desired}
$$

$$
RA = \frac{0x8180 \; \& \; 0x0080}{0x0080} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Recursion Available}
$$

**Step 4 — Extract RCODE (bits 0–3):**

$$
RCODE = 0x8180 \; \& \; 0x000F = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{No Error (NOERROR)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The value `0x8180` is the **most common Flags value** for a successful DNS response on the internet.

### Worked Example 2 — DNS Message Size for `www.ktu.edu.in`

We compute the total UDP payload size of a query for `www.ktu.edu.in`, type A, class IN.

**Step 1 — Fixed header:**

$$
H_{DNS} = 12 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2 — QNAME encoding (length-prefixed labels):**

$$
\text{www} \rightarrow [03][\text{w}][\text{w}][\text{w}] = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{ktu} \rightarrow [03][\text{k}][\text{t}][\text{u}] = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{edu} \rightarrow [03][\text{e}][\text{d}][\text{u}] = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{in} \rightarrow [02][\text{i}][\text{n}] = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{root} \rightarrow [00] = 1 \text{ byte (terminator)}
$$

$$
\text{QNAME}_{total} = 4 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 1 = 16 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Fixed question trailer:**

$$
Q_{fix} = QTYPE + QCLASS = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Total query size:**

$$
M_{query} = H_{DNS} + \text{QNAME}_{total} + Q_{fix} = 12 + 16 + 4 = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5 — Response payload (assuming one A answer with compression):**

$$
M_{resp} = 12 + 16 + 4 + \underbrace{(2 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 4)}_{RR_{A}=16} = 48 \text{ bytes}
$$

Both fit well within the **512-byte UDP limit**. ✅

### Worked Example 3 — Round-Trip Time (RTT) Calculation

Wireshark's `dns.time` field gives RTT directly. We can also derive:

$$
\Delta t_{DNS} = t_{response} - t_{query}
$$

For a query captured at $t_q = 14\!:\!22\!:\!10.345 \, \text{s}$ and response at $t_r = 14\!:\!22\!:\!10.397 \, \text{s}$:

$$
\Delta t_{DNS} = 0.397 - 0.345 = 0.052 \text{ s} = 52 \text{ ms}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Sub-Questions

| Question Type | What to Identify in Wireshark | Key Field |
|---|---|---|
| Is it query or response? | `dns.flags.response` | QR bit |
| Which server replied? | `dns.a`, `dns.aaaa` | Answer address |
| Was recursion used? | `dns.flags.authoritative` | AA bit |
| Was it truncated? | `dns.flags.truncated` | TC bit |
| What was queried? | `dns.qry.name` | QNAME |
| What is the TTL? | `dns.resp.ttl` | Cache lifetime |
| Transport used? | `udp.port == 53` or `tcp.port == 53` | L4 protocol |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DNS Resolution Flow (Recursive + Iterative Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client App: ping www.ktu.edu.in] --> B[OS Stub Resolver]
    B --> C{Is answer cached locally?}
    C -- Yes --> Z[Return cached IP to app]
    C -- No --> D[Recursive Resolver e.g. 8.8.8.8]
    D --> E[Root Server '.' via UDP 53]
    E -- Referral: .in NS --> D
    D --> F[TLD Server '.in']
    F -- Referral: edu.in NS --> D
    D --> G[Authoritative Server edu.in]
    G -- A record: 103.25.61.5 --> D
    D --> H[Cache answer with TTL 3600s]
    D --> I[Return IP to Client]
    I --> J[Client opens TCP connection]
```

## 4.2 DNS Packet Structure (Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7["Application Layer - DNS Message"]
        H1["Header - 12 bytes - ID, Flags, Counts"]
        Q1["Question Section - QNAME, QTYPE, QCLASS"]
        A1["Answer Section - RRs"]
        AU1["Authority Section - RRs"]
        AD1["Additional Section - RRs"]
    end
    subgraph L4["Transport Layer - UDP or TCP port 53"]
        UDP["UDP Header - 8 bytes - Src Port, Dst Port 53, Length, Checksum"]
    end
    subgraph L3["Network Layer - IP"]
        IP["IP Header - 20 bytes - Src IP, Dst IP, TTL, Protocol 17"]
    end
    subgraph L2["Data Link Layer - Ethernet"]
        ETH["Ethernet Header - Src MAC, Dst MAC, EtherType 0x0800"]
    end
    ETH --> IP --> UDP --> L7
```

## 4.3 Wireshark Capture & Filter Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Start Wireshark] --> S2[Select Interface e.g. eth0 or Wi-Fi]
    S2 --> S3[Apply Capture Filter: port 53]
    S3 --> S4[Open cmd: nslookup www.ktu.edu.in]
    S4 --> S5[Stop capture after response]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Display Filter: dns]
    S6 --> S7[Expand: Domain Name System node]
    S7 --> S8[Note: ID, Flags, Queries, Answers, TTL]
    S8 --> S9[Export packet as pcap for report]
```

## 4.4 DNS Header Flag-Bit Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F["Flags - 16 bits - MSB first"]
        B0["QR - 1 bit"]
        B14["OPCODE - 4 bits"]
        B58["AA TC RD RA - 4 bits"]
        B912["Z - 3 bits"]
        B12["AD CD - 2 bits"]
        B1315["RCODE - 4 bits"]
    end
    B0 --> B14 --> B58 --> B912 --> B12 --> B1315
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` 
**Identify the transport layer protocol and default port number used by DNS. Justify why TCP is also used occasionally.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DNS primarily uses **UDP on port 53** for queries because of low overhead — UDP is connectionless, has an **8-byte header** versus TCP's **20-byte minimum**, and supports the typical small query/response pattern.

However, TCP on port 53 is used in three cases:
1. **Zone transfers (AXFR/IXFR)** between primary and secondary name servers (RFC 5936).
2. When the response is **larger than 512 bytes** (e.g., DNSSEC, IPv6 glue, many answers) — the TC flag is set and the client retries via TCP.
3. When **truncated responses** are returned, signaling the client to re-query over TCP (RFC 7766).

Marks: UDP port 53 — 1 mark, reason for low overhead — 1 mark, TCP justification (3 cases) — 1 mark.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between recursive and iterative DNS queries with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Recursive Query | Iterative Query |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Resolver returns the **final answer** or an error | Server returns the **best referral** it has |
| Burden | On the **server** to resolve completely | On the **client/resolver** to chase referrals |
| Typical use | **Client → Resolver** | **Resolver → Root/TLD/Authoritative** |
| Example | `Host → 8.8.8.8` asking for `www.ktu.edu.in` | `.` root → returns `.in` TLD NS list |

Marks: 2 differences × 1 mark + 1 example — 1 mark.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Most students lose marks by **(a)** confusing the **Flags** field bit positions, **(b)** not labeling **hex values** with their binary conversion, **(c)** forgetting to mention **QCLASS = IN (1)** in the question section, and **(d)** not drawing a **proper labeled DNS header** diagram. Always quote **RFC 1035** for definitions and use **octet numbers** (0–11), not byte offsets alone.

---
