# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why `fork()` Enables Concurrency Without Threads

Let $P_0$ denote the parent process with a single listening socket $S$. When a datagram arrives, `recvfrom()` returns with:

- **Buffer** $B_c$ containing the client's request (e.g., the string `"GET TIME\n"`).
- **Source address** $A_c = (\text{IP}_c, \text{port}_c)$ stored in `struct sockaddr_in cli_addr`.

The server must perform two *independent* tasks:

1. Continue listening for the **next** client's datagram.
2. Compute and send the **time** to client $c$.

If performed sequentially in $P_0$, the time-to-serve the $(n+1)$-th client is:

$$
T_{\text{seq}}^{(n+1)} \;=\; T_{\text{recv}}^{(n+1)} + \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} T_{\text{compute}}^{(i)} + T_{\text{transmit}}^{(n+1)}
$$

After `fork()`, task (2) is delegated to a child $P_c$, so:

$$
T_{\text{conc}}^{(n+1)} \;=\; T_{\text{recv}}^{(n+1)} + \max\!\left(0,\; T_{\text{compute}}^{(n+1)} - \sum_{i \in \text{active}} T_{\text{remaining}}^{(i)}\right) + T_{\text{transmit}}^{(n+1)}
$$

For typical workloads where $T_{\text{compute}} \ll T_{\text{inter-arrival}}$, the response latency collapses to:

$$
T_{\text{conc}}^{(n+1)} \;\approx\; T_{\text{recv}}^{(n+1)} + T_{\text{transmit}}^{(n+1)} \quad \ll \quad T_{\text{seq}}^{(n+1)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The parent is never blocked by a slow client, so throughput scales **linearly** with the rate of incoming datagrams, modulo OS scheduling overhead.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Server Algorithm (Stepwise Exhaustive Derivation)

The following derivation shows *every* state transition in the server.

| Step | Statement (Verbatim from RFC-POSIX-Standard) | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **S1** | Create a UDP socket: `sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0);` | `AF_INET` = IPv4, `SOCK_DGRAM` = datagram, `0` = default protocol (UDP). |
| **S2** | Initialize `serv_addr` to zero: `bzero((char *)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));` | Avoids garbage bytes that may mis-match `bind`. |
| **S3** | Set `serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET;` | Mandatory address family field. |
| **S4** | Set `serv_addr.sin_addr.s_addr = INADDR_ANY;` | Server accepts datagrams on **any** of its local interfaces. |
| **S5** | Set `serv_addr.sin_port = htons(SERVER_PORT);` | `htons` converts host byte-order to **network byte-order (big-endian)**. |
| **S6** | `bind(sockfd, (struct sockaddr *)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));` | Assigns the address. **Returns 0** on success, `-1` (and sets `errno`) on failure. |
| **S7** | Enter an **infinite loop**: `while(1) { ... }` | Server runs until killed (SIGTERM/Ctrl+C). |
| **S8** | `n = recvfrom(sockfd, buffer, BUFSIZE, 0, (struct sockaddr *)&cli_addr, &cli_len);` | **Blocks** until a datagram arrives; `n` = bytes received; `cli_addr` = source. |
| **S9** | `cli_len = sizeof(cli_addr);` (set **before** `recvfrom` too, since kernel uses it as in/out) | POSIX requires `addrlen` to be initialized. |
| **S10** | `pid = fork();` | Creates child. Returns **0** in child, **child PID** in parent, **-1** on failure. |
| **S11a (child)** | If `pid == 0`: call `gettimeofday(&tv, NULL);` then `time(&raw);` then `localtime_r(&raw, &t_info);` then `strftime(...)` | Child computes the time string. |
| **S12a (child)** | `sendto(sockfd, time_str, strlen(time_str), 0, (struct sockaddr *)&cli_addr, cli_len);` | Sends time to **specific** client. |
| **S13a (child)** | `exit(0);` | Releases child's resources. |
| **S11b (parent)** | If `pid > 0`: continue loop (no `wait()` needed — children exit quickly; zombies reaped by `init` or `waitpid`). | Parent returns to `recvfrom()`. |
| **S11c (parent on error)** | If `pid < 0`: print `perror("fork failed")`; optionally `close(sockfd); exit(1);` | Handle resource exhaustion. |
| **S12** | Cleanup on shutdown: `close(sockfd);` | Releases the port. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `wait()` is omitted in the parent:** Because the child is *short-lived* and uses `exit(0)`, the kernel reaps it almost instantly. The parent *could* register a `SIGCHLD` handler with `waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG)` in a loop to be safe, but for a simple lab experiment, leaving the children to be reaped by `init` (or by the parent when it eventually exits) is acceptable. KTU lab exams **reward** mentioning `SIGCHLD` handling for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Client Algorithm (Stepwise Exhaustive)

| Step | Statement | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **C1** | `sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0);` | Create UDP socket. |
| **C2** | Zero & populate `serv_addr` with server IP and `htons(SERVER_PORT)`. | Target address. |
| **C3** | `sendto(sockfd, "GET TIME", strlen("GET TIME"), 0, (struct sockaddr *)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr));` | Fire-and-forget request. |
| **C4** | `n = recvfrom(sockfd, buffer, BUFSIZE, 0, NULL, NULL);` (NULL source addr if client doesn't care) | Waits for server's reply. |
| **C5** | `buffer[n] = '\0'; printf("Server time: %s\n", buffer);` | Display. |
| **C6** | `close(sockfd);` | Cleanup. |

> [!TIP]
> The client **does not need `bind()`** if it doesn't care which port the OS assigns (the kernel picks an **ephemeral port** automatically).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Time Conversion (Exhaustive)

Suppose `gettimeofday()` returns the following on **15-Jan-2025, 14:30:45.678901 IST**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{tv\_sec} &= 1\,736\,954\,445 \quad (\text{seconds since Epoch}) \\[4pt]
\text{tv\_usec} &= 678\,901 \quad (\text{microseconds within that second})
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 1 — Convert to `time_t`:
$$
t_{\text{raw}} = \text{time}(\text{NULL}) = 1\,736\,954\,445
$$

Step 2 — Convert to broken-down UTC time using `gmtime`:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{tm\_utc.tm\_year} &= 2025 - 1900 = 125 \\
\text{tm\_utc.tm\_mon}  &= 1 - 1 = 0 \quad (\text{January}) \\
\text{tm\_utc.tm\_mday} &= 15 \\
\text{tm\_utc.tm\_hour} &= 9 \quad (\text{14:30 IST} = 9:00 \text{ UTC, IST} = \text{UTC}+5{:}30) \\
\text{tm\_utc.tm\_min}  &= 0 \\
\text{tm\_utc.tm\_sec}  &= 45
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Apply `localtime` (IST, UTC+5:30):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{tm\_local.tm\_hour} &= 14 \\
\text{tm\_local.tm\_min}  &= 30 \\
\text{tm\_local.tm\_sec}  &= 45
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Format with `strftime(buf, 64, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S %Z", tm_local)`:
$$
S_{\text{out}} = \text{"2025-01-15 14:30:45 IST"}
$$

Step 5 — Send via `sendto`:
$$
\text{bytes\_sent} = \text{strlen}(S_{\text{out}}) = 24 \text{ bytes}
$$

This 24-byte payload is far below the **1472-byte** safe UDP limit, confirming UDP is the correct transport.

---

## 3.5 Edge-Case Analysis

| Scenario | Symptom | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| `bind()` fails with `EADDRINUSE` | Port already in use | Use `SO_REUSEADDR` via `setsockopt()` |
| `fork()` fails under high load | `-1` returned, `errno = EAGAIN` | Server logs error, retries on next iteration |
| Client never gets reply | Datagram lost | Client re-queries (acceptable for time service) |
| Time string truncated | Buffer too small | Use `BUFSIZE = 64` or larger |
| Child becomes zombie | Process table leak | Install `SIGCHLD` handler with `waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG)` in a loop |

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — End-to-End Time Request

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C1 as Client 1
    participant C2 as Client 2
    participant Srv as Server (Parent)
    participant Ch1 as Child Process 1
    participant Ch2 as Child Process 2
    participant OS as OS Kernel / Clock

    C1->>Srv: sendto("GET TIME", port=8080)
    Srv->>OS: recvfrom() blocks
    OS-->>Srv: datagram + cli_addr
    Srv->>Ch1: fork()
    Ch1->>OS: gettimeofday()
    OS-->>Ch1: timeval (sec, usec)
    Ch1->>Ch1: strftime format
    Ch1->>C1: sendto("2025-01-15 14:30:45")
    Ch1->>OS: exit(0)
    Srv->>OS: recvfrom() blocks again
    C2->>Srv: sendto("GET TIME", port=8080)
    OS-->>Srv: datagram + cli_addr
    Srv->>Ch2: fork()
    Ch2->>OS: gettimeofday()
    OS-->>Ch2: timeval (sec, usec)
    Ch2->>Ch2: strftime format
    Ch2->>C2: sendto("2025-01-15 14:30:46")
    Ch2->>OS: exit(0)
```

## 4.3 Process State Diagram — Fork & Exit Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Running: Server starts
    Running --> Blocked: recvfrom waits for datagram
    Blocked --> Running: datagram arrives
    Running --> Forking: fork syscall invoked
    Forking --> ChildActive: child created (pid == 0)
    Forking --> Running: parent continues (pid > 0)
    ChildActive --> ComputingTime: gettimeofday + strftime
    ComputingTime --> SendingReply: sendto client
    SendingReply --> Exiting: exit 0
    Exiting --> [*]: kernel reaps
    Running --> Blocked: next iteration recvfrom
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        direction TB
        Server[UDP Time Server\nIP 192.168.1.10\nPort 8080\nPID 1000]
        HostA[Client A\n192.168.1.20\nEphemeral Port]

    Server -->|UDP reply to ephemeral port| HostB
    Server -->|UDP reply to ephemeral port| HostC
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the **logical topology**, all three clients reach the same server IP and port. The server **distinguishes** them by the *source* address returned in `recvfrom()`. This is the entire reason UDP servers store `cli_addr` per request.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Distinguish between `connect()`-based and `connect()`-less UDP communication. Why does a UDP time server *not* call `connect()` on its socket?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A UDP socket that calls `connect()` fixes a *single* peer, allowing the use of `send()`/`recv()` instead of `sendto()`/`recvfrom()`. (1 mark)
- A **concurrent UDP time server** must handle *multiple* clients with one socket, so it cannot `connect()` to any single peer. (1 mark)
- Hence it uses `recvfrom()` to obtain each client's address dynamically, then `sendto(client_addr, ...)` to reply. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`** What is the role of the `struct timeval` structure in a UDP time server? Mention its two fields and their units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `struct timeval` holds the current time as returned by `gettimeofday()`. (1 mark)
- Field **`tv_sec`** stores the number of **seconds** elapsed since the **Unix Epoch** (`1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC`). (1 mark)
- Field **`tv_usec`** stores the additional **microseconds** ($0 \leq \text{tv\_usec} < 10^6$) within the current second. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget `htons()` on the port — this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> - Forgetting to call `bzero()` or `memset()` on `serv_addr` causes intermittent `bind()` failures — **1–2 marks lost**.
> - In UDP concurrent servers, students erroneously `close()` the socket in the child — this breaks the parent. **2-mark deduction**.
> - Neglecting `#include <arpa/inet.h>` causes `inet_pton` to be undeclared — **compilation penalty** even if logic is correct.

---

### Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **client-server model** for a UDP time service. Discuss why UDP is preferred over TCP for this service. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a block diagram showing:
   - One server with a single well-known port (e.g., 8080).
   - Multiple clients sending datagrams to that port.
   - Server spawning a child via `fork()` per datagram; child replies directly to the client's ephemeral port.

2. **Explanation of the model (2 marks):**
   - Server creates `SOCK_DGRAM` socket, binds to port 8080, enters `recvfrom()` loop.
   - On datagram arrival, server forks; child computes time and `sendto()`s the reply to the captured `cli_addr`.
   - Parent loops back; child exits.

3. **Why UDP is preferred (2 marks):**
   - **No handshake overhead** — saves round-trip latency.
   - **Stateless server** — easier to scale, no `accept()` queue, no half-open connections.
   - **Small request/reply** fits in one datagram; loss is non-fatal.
   - **Lower per-message overhead** (8-byte UDP header vs ≥20-byte TCP header).

**(b)** Write the **algorithm and key code segments** for the concurrent UDP time server. Explain the role of `fork()`, `recvfrom()`, and `sendto()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Algorithm (3 marks):**

   ```
   Step 1: Create UDP socket
   Step 2: Zero serv_addr; set family, port (htons), INADDR_ANY
   Step 3: bind socket to serv_addr
   Step 4: while (true):
       Step 4a: cli_len = sizeof(cli_addr)
       Step 4b: n = recvfrom(sockfd, buf, BUFSIZE, 0,
                              (struct sockaddr*)&cli_addr, &cli_len)
       Step 4c: pid = fork()
       Step 4c(i):  if (pid == 0):  // child
                       - gettimeofday(&tv, NULL)
                       - localtime_r(&tv, &t_info)
                       - strftime(time_str, ..., "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S", &t_info)
                       - sendto(sockfd, time_str, len, 0,
                                (struct sockaddr*)&cli_addr, cli_len)
                       - exit(0)
       Step 4c(ii): if (pid > 0):   // parent
                       - continue  // no wait() needed
       Step 4c(iii): if (pid < 0):  // error
                       - perror("fork")
   Step 5: close(sockfd)   // on shutdown
   ```

2. **Role of key system calls (4 marks):**
   - **`fork()`** — Duplicates the parent process. The child inherits the socket descriptor and `cli_addr`, allowing it to reply to the *specific* client while the parent continues accepting new datagrams. **(1.5 marks)**
   - **`recvfrom()`** — Blocks until a datagram arrives. Crucially, it *populates* `cli_addr` with the client's IP and port, which the child later uses in `sendto()`. **(1.5 marks)**
   - **`sendto()`** — Sends the formatted time string to the *exact* client address captured by `recvfrom()`. Without this, the server could not reply to the correct peer in a connectionless model. **(1 mark)**

---

### Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the **process-based concurrency model** used in a UDP time server. How does it differ from a **thread-based** model? Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of using `fork()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Process-based concurrency (3 marks):**
   - The server uses `fork()` to create a **separate process** for each incoming client request.
   - Each child has its **own address space**, file descriptor table, and PC.
   - The parent is **never blocked** by a child's compute/transmission, enabling high throughput.
   - Since UDP is stateless, the child can simply `sendto()` and `exit(0)`, releasing all resources.

2. **Comparison with thread-based model (2 marks):**

   | Aspect | `fork()` (Process) | `pthread_create()` (Thread) |
   |---|---|---|
   | Address space | **Separate** (copy-on-write) | **Shared** |
   | Creation cost | Higher (page-table duplication) | Lower |
   | IPC for shared data | Pipes, shared memory, signals | Direct global variable access |
   | Isolation / crash safety | **Stronger** (child crash ≠ parent) | Weaker (one thread crash may kill process) |

3. **One advantage & one disadvantage of `fork()` (2 marks):**
   - **Advantage:** **Isolation** — a buggy child cannot corrupt the parent's state; ideal for serving untrusted clients. **(1 mark)**
   - **Disadvantage:** **Resource cost** — each fork duplicates the address space; for thousands of clients per second, process creation overhead dominates and one switches to `select()`/`epoll()` or threads. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** Write the **client algorithm** that requests the current time from a UDP time server running on `127.0.0.1:8080`, prints the reply, and exits. Also explain why the client need **not** call `bind()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Client Algorithm (4 marks):**

   ```
   Step 1: sockfd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
   Step 2: bzero(&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr))
   Step 3: serv_addr.sin_family = AF_INET
           serv_addr.sin_port   = htons(8080)
           inet_pton(AF_INET, "127.0.0.1", &serv_addr.sin_addr)
   Step 4: sendto(sockfd, "GET TIME", 8, 0,
                  (struct sockaddr*)&serv_addr, sizeof(serv_addr))
   Step 5: n = recvfrom(sockfd, buf, BUFSIZE, 0, NULL, NULL)
   Step 6: buf[n] = '\0'
   Step 7: printf("Server time: %s\n", buf)
   Step 8: close(sockfd)
   ```

2. **Why the client does *not* call `bind()` (3 marks):**
   - The client has no well-known port; the server only needs to *reply* to whatever port the OS auto-assigned. **(1 mark)**
   - When `sendto()` is called without a prior `bind()`, the kernel **automatically** assigns an **ephemeral port** (typically in the range `32768–60999` on Linux) and binds the socket to the client's IP. **(1 mark)**
   - The server's `recvfrom()` captures this ephemeral port in `cli_addr.sin_port` and uses it in its `sendto()` reply, so the reply reaches the correct client socket even without an explicit `bind()`. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):**

- **UDP is connectionless** — no `accept()`, no `listen()`, no `connect()` on the server.
- **Socket creation triplet for UDP:** `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)`.
- **Always** wrap port in `htons()`; **always** zero `sockaddr_in` with `bzero()` or `memset()`.
- **Server flow:** `socket → bind → recvfrom (loop) → fork → [child: compute time → sendto → exit]`.
- **Client flow:** `socket → sendto (with serv_addr) → recvfrom → printf → close`.
- **`fork()` returns 0 in child, PID in parent, -1 on error** — handle all three branches.
- **Do NOT `close()` the socket in the child** of a UDP server (parent needs it for next `recvfrom()`).
- **`struct timeval`** has `tv_sec` (seconds) and `tv_usec` (microseconds).
- **Epoch** = `1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC`; `time_t` is seconds since Epoch.
- **Formatting chain:** `time_t → localtime → struct tm → strftime → char[]`.
- **Port 37** is the IANA-assigned time service port (rarely used in practice; labs use 8080/13/37).
- **Zombie prevention:** Install `SIGCHLD` handler calling `waitpid(-1, NULL, WNOHANG)` in a loop.
- **`SO_REUSEADDR`** solves "Address already in use" when restarting the server quickly.
- **Buffer size for time string:** `64 bytes` is safe.
- **KTU's favorite trick question:** "What happens if `bind()` is omitted on the server?" → The server gets a *random* port, making the service undiscoverable. The server **must** call `bind()`.
- **KTU's second favorite:** "Why use `gettimeofday()` instead of `time()`?" → Microsecond precision vs. whole-second precision.
- **Concurrency check:** After `fork()`, the **parent never blocks** on `wait()` for the child, ensuring high availability.
- **UDP header is 8 bytes**, TCP is ≥20 bytes — relevant for IoT/embedded viva questions.
- **`gettimeofday()`** returns `0` on success; `localtime()` returns a pointer to a *static* `struct tm` (use `localtime_r` in multi-threaded code for safety).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrent file server with PID response
Packet capturing using raw sockets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL507 - NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Packet capturing using raw sockets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Raw Socket**: A type of socket (communication endpoint) that allows direct access to the underlying transport and network layer protocols of the Internet Protocol suite. Unlike `SOCK_STREAM` (TCP) and `SOCK_DGRAM` (UDP) sockets, **raw sockets bypass the kernel's automatic protocol processing**, enabling a userspace program to read, construct, and inject packets at the IP layer.

**Packet Capturing** is the process of intercepting and logging data packets that traverse a computer network. Using raw sockets, a process can passively **sniff** every packet that the host receives (or sends) — making it the foundation of network analyzers such as `Wireshark`, `tcpdump`, and `ping`.

The system call signature used in Linux is:

```c
int sockfd = socket(int domain, int type, int protocol);
```

For raw sockets, `type = SOCK_RAW` and `protocol` specifies the next-header identifier (e.g., `IPPROTO_TCP = 6`, `IPPROTO_UDP = 17`, `IPPROTO_ICMP = 1`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Transparent Envelope" Post Office

Imagine two post offices:
1. **Normal Mailroom (TCP/UDP Socket)**: The postmaster opens every envelope, reads the outer label, delivers the letter, and **never lets you see the envelope itself**.
2. **Forensic Post Office (Raw Socket)**: Every envelope arriving at the building is **laid out on a glass table**, still sealed, and you — the network detective — are allowed to inspect the **outer envelope (IP header)**, the **inner letter cover (TCP/UDP/ICMP header)**, and even **peek at the letter inside (payload)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Raw sockets provide *read* access to the IP header, while *write* access allows the application to construct custom IP headers. This is why raw sockets are **privileged operations** in Linux (require `CAP_NET_RAW` or root) and are entirely restricted for non-administrative users in modern Windows.

## 1.3 Why Raw Sockets? — Engineering Motivation

| Application | Why Raw Socket? |
|---|---|
| `ping` | Builds custom **ICMP Echo Request** packets |
| `traceroute` | Manipulates **TTL field** to provoke ICMP Time Exceeded |
| `Wireshark / tcpdump` | Sniffs all incoming/outgoing traffic for analysis |
| Custom Routing Protocols | Implements protocols **not natively supported** by the OS |
| Network Security / IDS | Inspects malicious packets **before the OS reacts** |

> [!TIP]
> Standard metrics used: MTU = **1500 bytes** (Ethernet), IP header length = **20 bytes** (no options), max IP datagram = **65535 bytes**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Socket Types in Linux

A Unix/Linux network socket is a *file descriptor* bound to a transport protocol. The `type` parameter in the `socket()` call dictates the behavior:

| Socket Type | Macro | Protocol Header Built By | Header Visible to User? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stream Socket | `SOCK_STREAM` | Kernel (TCP) | ❌ No |
| Datagram Socket | `SOCK_DGRAM` | Kernel (UDP) | ❌ No |
| **Raw Socket** | **`SOCK_RAW`** | **User (Manual)** | ✅ **Yes (IP + L4)** |

> [!NOTE]
> In **Linux**, raw sockets receive packets *starting from the IP header* — the **Ethernet frame header is stripped by the kernel NIC driver**. To capture the full Ethernet frame, one must either use `PACKET` sockets (`AF_PACKET`) or the **`libpcap`** library.

## 2.2 Protocol Stack Position of a Raw Socket

A raw socket "taps" the **IP layer** from above. Conceptually:

```text
Application (User Code: read() / write())
        ▲   ▼
      ┌─────────────┐
      │  RAW SOCKET  │  ◄── userspace interface to IP layer
      └─────────────┘
        ▲   ▼
   ┌──────────────┐
   │  IP Layer    │  (kernel networking subsystem)
   └──────────────┘
        ▲   ▼
   ┌──────────────┐
   │  NIC Driver  │  (delivers packets up from hardware)
   └──────────────┘
```

> [!WARNING]
> On receiving a packet via `recvfrom()` on a raw socket, the buffer contains: **`[IP Header][L4 Header][Payload]`**. The Ethernet trailer and CRC are **not delivered** to userspace.

## 2.3 Core Data Structures (Linux `<netinet/*.h>` headers)

### A. `struct iphdr` — The IPv4 Header (20 bytes minimum)

| Field (C name) | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `version` + `ihl` | 8 | Version (=4) + Header length in 32-bit words |
| `tos` | 8 | Type of Service / DSCP |
| `tot_len` | 16 | **Total datagram length (header + data)** |
| `id` | 16 | Fragment identification |
| `frag_off` | 16 | Fragment offset + flags |
| `ttl` | 8 | **Time To Live — decremented per hop** |
| `protocol` | 8 | Next-protocol identifier (1=ICMP, 6=TCP, 17=UDP) |
| `check` | 16 | **Header checksum** |
| `saddr` | 32 | Source IP address (network byte order) |
| `daddr` | 32 | Destination IP address (network byte order) |

### B. `struct tcphdr` — The TCP Header (20 bytes minimum)

| Field | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `source` | 16 | Source port |
| `dest` | 16 | Destination port |
| `seq` | 32 | Sequence number |
| `ack_seq` | 32 | Acknowledgment number |
| `doff` | 4 | Data offset (header length in 32-bit words) |
| `urg_ack_psh_rst_syn_fin` | 6×1 | TCP flags |
| `window` | 16 | Sliding window size |
| `check` | 16 | TCP checksum (covers pseudo-header + TCP + data) |
| `urg_ptr` | 16 | Urgent pointer |

### C. `struct icmphdr` — The ICMP Header (8 bytes)

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `type` | 1 byte | ICMP message type (8 = Echo Request, 0 = Echo Reply) |
| `code` | 1 byte | Sub-type code |
| `checksum` | 2 bytes | ICMP message checksum |
| `un.echo.sequence` | 2 bytes | Sequence number (for ping) |
| `un.echo.id` | 2 bytes | Process identifier (for ping) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are the equations and constants **required for the Networks Lab viva and university exam** on raw sockets and the IP/ICMP/TCP checksum.

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **IPv4 Header Size** | $H_{IP} = 4 \times \text{ihl}$ | Minimum 20 bytes; ihl in 32-bit words |
| **IP Header Checksum** | $C_{IP} = \overline{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i}$ | One's-complement of 16-bit word sum |
| **TCP Pseudo-Header Checksum** | $C_{TCP} = \overline{\sum w_{ph} + \sum w_{TCP} + \sum w_{data}}$ | Covers Src/Dst IP, Protocol, TCP Length, TCP, Data |
| **ICMP Checksum** | $C_{ICMP} = \overline{\sum w_{ICMP}}$ | Same as IP checksum algorithm |
| **Max IP Datagram** | $L_{max} = 2^{16} - 1 = 65535$ bytes | Theoretical max |
| **Ethernet MTU** | $MTU = 1500$ bytes | Standard link MTU |
| **Network Byte Order** | Big-endian | Use `htons()`, `htonl()`, `ntohs()`, `ntohl()` |
| **TCP Header Min Size** | 20 bytes | With no options |
| **ICMP Echo Header Size** | 8 bytes | Plus optional payload (e.g., 56 bytes for ping) |
| **TTL Initial Value** | Recommended: **64** (Linux) | Decremented at every router |

> [!NOTE]
> **Checksum Algorithm (pseudo-code for viva)**:
> 1. Treat the header as a sequence of 16-bit words.
> 2. Sum all 16-bit words using 32-bit accumulator, **with wrap-around carry** (i.e., $s = s + w + \text{carry}_{\text{out}}$).
> 3. Fold any carry bits back: $\text{if } s > 0xFFFF \text{ then } s = (s \; \&\; 0xFFFF) + (s >> 16)$.
> 4. Final checksum = bitwise NOT of `s`: $C = \overline{s}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Raw sockets are the **swiss-army knife** of network engineering:
- **Network telemetry & SLA monitoring** — operators build synthetic probes (ping, HTTP GET) using raw ICMP/TCP.
- **Software Defined Networking (SDN)** — controllers like Open vSwitch use raw sockets to send/receive control packets.
- **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** — virtual firewalls and NAT gateways often use raw sockets for packet mangling.
- **Cybersecurity penetration testing** — tools like `nmap` and `hping3` rely on raw sockets to craft custom probes.

> [!TIP]
> For **Wireshark-style frame capture including Ethernet headers**, prefer `AF_PACKET` sockets or the **libpcap** library. The `tcpdump` source code is a famous reference implementation for raw-socket packet capture.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Algorithm Walk-Through & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Operational Workflow of a Packet-Capture Raw Socket Program

The lifecycle of a raw-socket packet sniffer has **6 deterministic steps**. Every KTU lab exam answer for this experiment should articulate these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six-Stage Lifecycle of a Raw-Socket Sniffer**:
> 1. **Create** the raw socket via `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, protocol)`.
> 2. **Bind** (optional) to a local IP address.
> 3. **Receive** packets in an infinite loop via `recvfrom()`.
> 4. **Parse** the IP header by casting the buffer to `struct iphdr *`.
> 5. **Inspect** the L4 header (TCP/UDP/ICMP) using the `protocol` field of the IP header.
> 6. **Display** source/destination IPs, ports, and protocol type; close socket on exit.

## 3.2 Reference Implementation (Linux, GCC)

The following C program is a **complete, compilable raw-socket sniffer** as expected in a KTU lab record. It captures all TCP packets and prints source/destination IPs.

```c
/* raw_sniffer.c — Packet capture using raw sockets (Linux) */
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <netinet/ip.h>
#include <netinet/tcp.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>

int main(void) {
    int rawsock;
    ssize_t n;
    unsigned char buffer[65536];
    struct sockaddr_in src;
    socklen_t srclen;

    /* Step 1: Create raw socket that receives TCP segments */
    rawsock = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, IPPROTO_TCP);
    if (rawsock < 0) {
        perror("socket() failed — run as root");
        return EXIT_FAILURE;
    }

    /* Step 2: Continuously receive packets */
    for (;;) {
        srclen = sizeof(src);
        n = recvfrom(rawsock, buffer, sizeof(buffer), 0,
                     (struct sockaddr *)&src, &srclen);
        if (n < 0) { perror("recvfrom"); break; }

        /* Step 3: Cast buffer to IP header */
        struct iphdr *ip = (struct iphdr *)buffer;

        /* Step 4: Extract addresses (already in network byte order) */
        struct in_addr saddr = { ip->saddr };
        struct in_addr daddr = { ip->daddr };

        printf("---- TCP Packet Captured ----\n");
        printf("Source IP      : %s\n",  inet_ntoa(saddr));
        printf("Destination IP : %s\n",  inet_ntoa(daddr));
        printf("IP Total Length: %u bytes\n", ntohs(ip->tot_len));
        printf("TTL            : %u\n",        ip->ttl);
        printf("Protocol       : TCP\n");

        /* Step 5: Parse TCP header (offset = IP header length) */
        struct tcphdr *tcp = (struct tcphdr *)(buffer + (ip->ihl * 4));
        printf("Src Port       : %u\n", ntohs(tcp->source));
        printf("Dst Port       : %u\n", ntohs(tcp->dest));
        printf("Seq Number     : %u\n", ntohl(tcp->seq));
        printf("Flags          : 0x%02X\n", tcp->fin + (tcp->syn << 1)
                                          + (tcp->rst << 2) + (tcp->psh << 3)
                                          + (tcp->ack << 4) + (tcp->urg << 5));
        printf("----------------------------\n\n");
    }
    close(rawsock);
    return EXIT_SUCCESS;
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Compile with**: `gcc raw_sniffer.c -o raw_sniffer` **and run as**: `sudo ./raw_sniffer`
> Failure to use `sudo` will produce `socket(): Operation not permitted` because raw sockets require the `CAP_NET_RAW` capability.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: IP Header Checksum

The IP checksum protects the IP header against bit-errors. The standard is **RFC 1071**. We derive it below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The receiver must compute the checksum on the received header (with the `check` field set to 0) and verify it equals 0. If yes, the header is intact.

### Derivation (Exhaustive)

Let the IP header be divided into $N$ consecutive **16-bit words** $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{N-1}$.

**Step 1** — Sum all words in a 32-bit accumulator, propagating carries:

$$
S_0 = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i
$$

**Step 2** — Fold the high 16 bits of the accumulator back into the low 16 bits (since the sum may exceed 16 bits):

$$
S_1 = (S_0 \;\&\; 0xFFFF) + (S_0 >> 16)
$$

Repeat this folding until $S \le 0xFFFF$ (at most 2 iterations suffice).

**Step 3** — Take the one's complement (bitwise NOT):

$$
C = \overline{S_1}
$$

This $C$ is the IP header checksum field. The sender stores $C$; the receiver computes the same sum over the *received* header (with checksum field zeroed) — if correct, the result is 0.

### Worked Numerical Example (8-word header)

Suppose the header has 8 words (16 bytes) with the checksum field set to 0 during computation:

$$
w_0 = 0x4500, \quad w_1 = 0x003C, \quad w_2 = 0x1C46, \quad w_3 = 0x4000,
\quad w_4 = 0x4006, \quad w_5 = 0xB112, \quad w_6 = 0xAC10, \quad w_7 = 0x0A63
$$

**Step 1 — Sum**:

$$
S_0 = 0x4500 + 0x003C + 0x1C46 + 0x4000 + 0x4006 + 0xB112 + 0xAC10 + 0x0A63
$$

Computing term by term in hexadecimal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x4500 + 0x003C &= 0x453C \\
0x453C + 0x1C46 &= 0x6182 \\
0x6182 + 0x4000 &= 0xA182 \\
0xA182 + 0x4006 &= 0xE188 \\
0xE188 + 0xB112 &= 0x1929A \\
0x1929A + 0xAC10 &= 0x23EAA \\
0x23EAA + 0x0A63 &= 0x2490D
\end{aligned}
$$

So $S_0 = 0x2490D$. This exceeds 16 bits (carry $0x2$ in the high word).

**Step 2 — Fold carry**:

$$
S_1 = (0x2490D \;\&\; 0xFFFF) + (0x2490D >> 16) = 0x490D + 0x0002 = 0x490F
$$

**Step 3 — One's complement**:

$$
C = \overline{0x490F} = 0xB6F0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification**: At the receiver, after replacing the checksum field with 0, the sum would become $S_1 + 0xB6F0 = 0xFFFF$. After one's complement, the result is $0$, confirming header integrity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How the Program Handles Each Protocol

| IP `protocol` Field | L4 Header Struct | Port Fields? | Useful For |
|---|---|---|---|
| `IPPROTO_ICMP (1)` | `struct icmphdr` | ❌ No | `ping`, traceroute |
| `IPPROTO_TCP (6)` | `struct tcphdr` | ✅ Yes | HTTP, SSH, FTP analysis |
| `IPPROTO_UDP (17)` | `struct udphdr` | ✅ Yes | DNS, VoIP, video streaming |
| Other | Generic `unsigned char*` | ❌ | IGMP, OSPF, GRE tunneling |

## 3.5 Sample Lab Viva — Expected Output

When run on a Linux host that is browsing a webpage, the program prints:

```text
---- TCP Packet Captured ----
Source IP      : 192.168.1.10
Destination IP : 142.250.193.46
IP Total Length: 60 bytes
TTL            : 64
Protocol       : TCP
Src Port       : 54312
Dst Port       : 443
Seq Number     : 1234567890
Flags          : 0x18
----------------------------
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Flags value `0x18 = 0b00011000` corresponds to `PSH=1, ACK=1` — typical of a data-carrying segment in an established TCP connection (e.g., an HTTPS request).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Flow — How a Packet Travels From the Wire to User Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Network Cable Ethernet Frame arrives at NIC"] --> B["NIC Driver copies frame into kernel socket buffer sk_buff"]
    B --> C["Kernel strips Ethernet header and CRC"]
    C --> D["Kernel parses IP header and dispatches to protocol handler via ip protos"]
    D --> E{"IP header protocol field"}
    E -- "TCP 6" --> F1["TCP handler"]
    E -- "UDP 17" --> F2["UDP handler"]
    E -- "ICMP 1" --> F3["ICMP handler"]
    F1 --> G1["Deliver a copy of packet to all matching raw sockets"]
    F2 --> G2["Deliver a copy of packet to all matching raw sockets"]
    F3 --> G3["Deliver a copy of packet to all matching raw sockets"]
    G1 --> H["Application calls recvfrom and buffer is filled with IP plus L4 plus payload"]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I["Application casts buffer to struct iphdr and parses"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight**: Multiple applications can open raw sockets on the same protocol — each will receive its own **copy** of every matching packet. This is called **packet fan-out** and is the basis for network analyzers running concurrently.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Captured Packet in `buffer[]`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph buffer["User Buffer 65536 bytes"]
        direction LR
        P1["Byte 0 - Byte 19: IP Header struct iphdr 20 bytes"] --> P2["Byte 20 - Byte 39: TCP Header struct tcphdr 20 bytes"] --> P3["Byte 40 - Byte N: TCP Payload N minus 40 bytes"] --> P4["Byte N plus 1 - End: Padding or extra bytes"]
    end
    buffer --> CAST["Cast buffer to struct iphdr ip for IP parsing"]
    buffer --> TCP["Cast buffer plus ip ihl times 4 to struct tcphdr tcp for TCP parsing"]
    buffer --> PAY["Cast buffer plus ip ihl times 4 plus tcp doff times 4 to char payload for data parsing"]
```

## 4.3 System Architecture — The Lab Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LAB["Linux Lab Machine KTU Networks Lab"]
        direction TB
        SRC["Traffic Generator e.g. ping to 8.8.8.8"] --> NIC1["NIC eth0"]
        NIC1 --> K1["Kernel IP Stack"]
        K1 --> RS["Raw Socket SOCK RAW IPPROTO TCP or ICMP"]
        RS --> APP["C Program raw sniffer c"]
        APP --> OUT["stdout: IP, Ports, Flags, Length"]
    end
    NIC1 -. "Optionally" .-> PCAP["Wireshark captures for verification"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Protocol to Sniff?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"What kind of traffic do you want to inspect?"} -- "Web, SSH, FTP" --> A1["Use IPPROTO TCP and cast to struct tcphdr"]
    Q1 -- "DNS, VoIP, video" --> A2["Use IPPROTO UDP and cast to struct udphdr"]
    Q1 -- "ping, traceroute" --> A3["Use IPPROTO ICMP and cast to struct icmphdr"]
    Q1 -- "All IP traffic" --> A4["Use htons ETH_P_IP via AF_PACKET socket and manually parse IP header"]
    A1 --> RECV["recvfrom populates buffer with IP plus TCP plus payload"]
    A2 --> RECV
    A3 --> RECV
    A4 --> RECV
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between `SOCK_STREAM`, `SOCK_DGRAM`, and `SOCK_RAW` socket types with one example use-case for each.**

**Model Answer**:

| Type | Transport | Connection | Kernel Action | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `SOCK_STREAM` | TCP | Connection-oriented | Full header built by kernel | Web browser → server |
| `SOCK_DGRAM` | UDP | Connectionless | Header built by kernel | DNS resolver |
| `SOCK_RAW` | User-defined | None | Header built by **application** | Building ICMP ping packets |

> [!TIP]
> The single defining feature of `SOCK_RAW` is that the **application is responsible for constructing the transport-layer (and sometimes the IP) header**, while the kernel does NOT touch it.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why do raw sockets require administrator (root) privileges in Linux? Mention any two real-world applications of raw sockets.**

**Model Answer**:

Raw sockets are privileged because they allow a process to:
1. **Inject arbitrary packets** into the network, bypassing kernel security policy (e.g., spoof source IP).
2. **Sniff all incoming traffic** — a clear privacy violation if misused.
3. **Manipulate low-level protocol fields** (TTL, flags) that affect routing and firewall rules.

Hence, only processes with the `CAP_NET_RAW` capability (or UID 0) can create them.

**Two real-world applications**:
- `ping` utility — uses `IPPROTO_ICMP` raw socket to send Echo Requests.
- `Wireshark / tcpdump` — uses `AF_PACKET` / raw sockets to capture all packets for analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of the IPv4 header with a neat diagram. List the fields whose values are modified by routers during packet forwarding.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** Write a C program using **raw sockets** to capture and display the **source IP, destination IP, and protocol** of every incoming packet. Mention the necessary compile and execution privileges.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

The IPv4 header is **20 bytes** (no options) divided into the following fields:

| Field | Bits | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Version | 4 | IP version (always 4) |
| IHL | 4 | Header length in 32-bit words |
| DSCP / ECN | 8 | QoS marking |
| Total Length | 16 | Entire datagram size in bytes |
| Identification | 16 | Used for fragmentation |
| Flags + Fragment Offset | 16 | DF, MF bits + offset |
| TTL | 8 | Decremented per hop |
| Protocol | 8 | Next header (1, 6, 17, …) |
| Header Checksum | 16 | Recomputed at every hop |
| Source Address | 32 | Sender's IP |
| Destination Address | 32 | Receiver's IP |

**Fields modified by routers**:
- **TTL** — decremented by 1 at every hop.
- **Header Checksum** — recomputed after TTL decrement.
- (Options, if present, may be processed and stripped.)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <sys/socket.h>
#include <netinet/in.h>
#include <netinet/ip.h>
#include <arpa/inet.h>

int main(void) {
    int s = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, IPPROTO_TCP);
    if (s < 0) { perror("socket"); return 1; }

    unsigned char buf[65536];
    while (1) {
        ssize_t n = recv(s, buf, sizeof(buf), 0);
        if (n < 0) continue;
        struct iphdr *ip = (struct iphdr *)buf;
        struct in_addr sa = { ip->saddr }, da = { ip->daddr };
        printf("Src: %-15s  Dst: %-15s  Proto: %u  Len: %u\n",
               inet_ntoa(sa), inet_ntoa(da),
               (unsigned)ip->protocol, (unsigned)ntohs(ip->tot_len));
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Compile**: `gcc raw.c -o raw`
**Run**: `sudo ./raw` (root privilege mandatory for `SOCK_RAW`).

---

### **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Internet Checksum algorithm** used in the IP header. Derive the checksum for a 4-word header: $0x4500$, $0x003C$, $0x1C46$, $0x4000$ (with the checksum field itself set to zero during computation).
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **raw sockets** with **libpcap** for packet capture. State two situations where raw sockets are preferred and two where libpcap is preferred.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

The Internet Checksum is the **one's-complement of the one's-complement sum of all 16-bit words in the header**.

**Step 1 — Sum**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= 0x4500 + 0x003C + 0x1C46 + 0x4000 \\
    &= 0x453C + 0x1C46 + 0x4000 \\
    &= 0x6182 + 0x4000 \\
    &= 0xA182
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Fold carry**: $S_0 = 0xA182 < 0xFFFF$, so no carry to fold; $S_1 = 0xA182$.

**Step 3 — One's complement**: $C = \overline{0xA182} = 0x5E7D$.

> **Final checksum** = `0x5E7D`.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

| Feature | Raw Socket | libpcap |
|---|---|---|
| Captures Ethernet frame? | ❌ No (IP onwards) | ✅ Yes |
| Promiscuous mode? | Manual via `ioctl` | One call: `pcap_set_promisc` |
| Cross-platform? | Linux-centric | Linux/Windows/macOS/BSD |
| Filtering language | None (manual C code) | BPF (`tcpdump` syntax) |
| Ease of use | Low | High |

**Prefer raw sockets when**:
1. Building custom **probe packets** (e.g., `ping`, `traceroute`).
2. Embedding packet injection inside a kernel-bypass engine.

**Prefer libpcap when**:
1. Cross-platform **GUI/network analyzers** (Wireshark uses libpcap).
2. Need **BPF filtering** to capture only specific traffic efficiently.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> - **Forgetting `#include <netinet/ip.h>`** — the C program will not compile. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> - **Forgetting `htons()` / `ntohs()`** — the displayed port number / length will be a meaningless integer. This is the #1 reason for zero marks in lab viva.
> - **Forgetting root privilege** — Students who run `./raw` without `sudo` report `socket() failed: Operation not permitted` and conclude the code is wrong. Mentioning `CAP_NET_RAW` or `sudo` in viva is mandatory.
> - **Confusing AF_PACKET vs SOCK_RAW** — `AF_PACKET` captures the **Ethernet header**; `SOCK_RAW` (over `AF_INET`) does **NOT**.
> - **Checksum hand-calculation errors** — ensure that the carry-fold step is performed **before** the one's complement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Lab Exam & Viva**

- ✅ A **raw socket** is created with `socket(AF_INET, SOCK_RAW, protocol)` where `protocol` ∈ {`IPPROTO_ICMP`, `IPPROTO_TCP`, `IPPROTO_UDP`}.
- ✅ Raw sockets provide **read access to the IP header** in received packets.
- ✅ Linux raw sockets **do NOT include the Ethernet header** — use `AF_PACKET` or `libpcap` for that.
- ✅ Raw sockets require **root / `CAP_NET_RAW` privilege** in Linux.
- ✅ The IP header is **20 bytes** minimum; the total length field reports the **full datagram size**.
- ✅ **TTL is decremented by every router**; if it reaches 0, the packet is dropped and an ICMP "Time Exceeded" is sent.
- ✅ The **Internet Checksum** is the one's complement of the 16-bit one's-complement sum of the header; the receiver verifies by recomputing and checking the result is `0x0000` (or `0xFFFF` before NOT).
- ✅ **Network byte order is big-endian**; use `htonl` / `htons` for sending and `ntohl` / `ntohs` for receiving.
- ✅ Typical values: **TTL = 64** (Linux), **MTU = 1500** (Ethernet), **IP header min = 20 B**, **ICMP echo header = 8 B**.
- ✅ On `recvfrom()` the buffer contains **IP header + L4 header + payload**, in that order.
- ✅ `Wireshark`, `tcpdump`, `ping`, and `traceroute` are the four canonical raw-socket applications.
- ✅ Linux declares raw socket structures in `<netinet/ip.h>`, `<netinet/tcp.h>`, `<netinet/udp.h>`, `<netinet/ip_icmp.h>`.
- ✅ Compile with `gcc file.c -o file`, run with `sudo ./file`.
- ✅ The `protocol` field of the IP header determines which `struct *hdr` to cast the buffer to.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary for last-minute revision**:
> *"A raw socket is a privileged, kernel-bypass interface to the IP layer that hands applications the IP header and transport header, letting them sniff and forge packets directly — but it excludes the Ethernet header and demands root."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarizing router commands (modes, configuration, routing table, interfaces)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCSL507 - NETWORKS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarizing router commands (modes, configuration, routing table, interfaces)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Router** is a Layer 3 (Network Layer) internetworking device that operates on the OSI model's Network Layer and uses logical **IP addresses** to forward data packets between different computer networks. The router is governed by its proprietary operating system called **Cisco IOS (Internetwork Operating System)**, which provides a Command Line Interface (CLI) for configuration, monitoring, and troubleshooting.

**Router modes** refer to the hierarchical execution contexts in the Cisco IOS CLI that determine the set of commands available to a user at any given time. Each mode serves a specific administrative function and is protected by a distinct prompt.

The fundamental operational modes of a Cisco router are:

1. **User EXEC Mode** – Read-only monitoring mode (Prompt: `Router>`)
2. **Privileged EXEC Mode** – Full read-write administrative mode (Prompt: `Router#`)
3. **Global Configuration Mode** – Top-level configuration context (Prompt: `Router(config)#`)
4. **Interface Configuration Mode** – Per-interface configuration (Prompt: `Router(config-if)#`)
5. **Sub-interface Configuration Mode** – For logical sub-interfaces (Prompt: `Router(config-subif)#`)
6. **Line Configuration Mode** – For console, VTY, AUX lines (Prompt: `Router(config-line)#`)
7. **Router Configuration Mode** – For routing protocol processes (Prompt: `Router(config-router)#`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCSL507):** Module Experiment 2 specifically requires students to *demonstrate practical familiarity* with the router's IOS modes, basic system configuration, interface IP assignment, and inspection of the routing table using commands like `show ip route`, `show ip interface brief`, `show running-config`, and `show version`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Reception" Model

Think of a **Cisco Router** as a large international **Hotel** with thousands of rooms (network interfaces) and guests (data packets):

- The **User EXEC Mode** is like a **visitor standing at the lobby** — they can look at the hotel directory, ask basic questions, and view public information, but cannot enter any office or change room assignments.
- The **Privileged EXEC Mode** is like the **Front Desk Manager** — they have the master key. They can reconfigure rooms, view the guest register, and access restricted areas.
- The **Global Configuration Mode** is the **General Manager's Office** — all major hotel-wide policies (e.g., new Wi-Fi password, building name) are set here. From here, managers can be sent out to specific departments.
- The **Interface Configuration Mode** is the **Housekeeping Supervisor for a specific floor** — they only handle room details (e.g., Room 301 gets a king bed, Room 302 gets twin beds).
- The **Line Configuration Mode** is the **Telephone Operator's station** — responsible for setting up how calls (connections via console, telnet, SSH) come into the hotel.
- The **Router Configuration Mode** is the **Travel Agency Counter** — handles routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, EIGRP) that decide which "roads" guests use to reach other hotels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard IOS Prompts Quick Reference:**
>
> | Mode | Prompt Symbol | Analogy |
> |------|---------------|---------|
> | User EXEC | `>` | Hotel Visitor |
> | Privileged EXEC | `#` | Front Desk Manager |
> | Global Config | `(config)#` | General Manager |
> | Interface Config | `(config-if)#` | Floor Supervisor |
> | Sub-interface | `(config-subif)#` | Sub-floor Supervisor |
> | Line Config | `(config-line)#` | Telephone Operator |
> | Router Config | `(config-router)#` | Travel Agency |

## 1.3 Routing Table — Definition

The **Routing Table** is a data structure stored in the router's memory (RAM) that maintains a list of known network destinations, the associated metrics, the next-hop IP address, and the outgoing interface through which packets destined for those networks should be forwarded.

## 1.4 Router Interfaces — Definition

Router **interfaces** are the physical or logical ports through which the router connects to networks. Common physical interfaces include:

- **FastEthernet (Fa0/0, Fa0/1)** — 100 Mbps copper Ethernet
- **GigabitEthernet (Gi0/0, Gi0/1)** — 1 Gbps Ethernet
- **Serial (S0/0/0, S0/0/1)** — WAN serial interface (for point-to-point links)
- **Loopback (Lo0)** — Virtual/logical interface, always UP, used for management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Interface Naming Convention:** `Interface-type slot/module/port`
> Example: `GigabitEthernet0/0/0` = Type=GigabitEthernet, Slot=0, Module=0, Port=0
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Command Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Mode Transition Logic

The router modes form a strict parent-child hierarchy. The transition follows a one-way "drill-down" path, and you must "exit" back up to return to a previous mode.

```
Router>  (User EXEC)
   |
   | enable
   v
Router#  (Privileged EXEC)
   |
   | configure terminal
   v
Router(config)#  (Global Configuration)
   |
   |--- interface fa0/0 ----> Router(config-if)#  (Interface Config)
   |
   |--- router rip -------> Router(config-router)# (Router Config)
   |
   |--- line console 0 ---> Router(config-line)# (Line Config)
```

### 2.1.1 Mode-by-Mode Functional Breakdown

**1. User EXEC Mode (`Router>`)**
- Default mode after login
- Limited commands: `ping`, `traceroute`, `show clock`, `enable`
- **Cannot** modify configuration

**2. Privileged EXEC Mode (`Router#`)**
- Access by typing `enable` from User EXEC
- Full read access via `show` commands
- Can reload router, erase configuration, enter configuration mode
- Key commands: `show running-config`, `show ip route`, `show ip interface brief`, `show version`, `copy running-config startup-config`

**3. Global Configuration Mode (`Router(config)#`)**
- Access via `configure terminal` (or `conf t`)
- Commands issued here affect the **entire router**
- Key commands: `hostname`, `enable secret`, `banner motd`, `ip routing`, `line`, `interface`

**4. Interface Configuration Mode (`Router(config-if)#`)**
- Access via `interface <type><slot/module/port>` from global config
- Commands here affect **only that specific interface**
- Key commands: `ip address <IP> <MASK>`, `no shutdown`, `description`, `clock rate` (for serial DCE), `bandwidth`

**5. Sub-interface Configuration Mode (`Router(config-subif)#`)**
- Access via `interface <type><slot/module/port>.<sub>` (e.g., `fa0/0.10`)
- Logical subdivision of a physical interface, used in **Router-on-a-Stick (Inter-VLAN routing)**

**6. Line Configuration Mode (`Router(config-line)#`)**
- Access via `line console 0`, `line vty 0 4`, or `line aux 0`
- Configures login authentication, timeouts, and transport protocols
- Key commands: `password`, `login`, `exec-timeout`, `transport input ssh telnet`

**7. Router Configuration Mode (`Router(config-router)#`)**
- Access via `router <protocol>` (e.g., `router rip`, `router ospf 1`)
- Activates and configures a routing protocol process

## 2.2 The Boot Sequence (Why Modes Exist)

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding the boot sequence is essential because it explains the role of each configuration mode:
> 1. **POST (Power-On Self-Test)** — Hardware diagnostic
> 2. **Load Bootstrap from ROM** — Initiates IOS loading
> 3. **Locate IOS** — Searches flash memory
> 4. **Load IOS into RAM** — IOS becomes active
> 5. **Locate Startup-Configuration** — Searches NVRAM
> 6. **Load Startup-Config into RAM as Running-Config** — The active configuration
> 7. **Enter User EXEC Mode** — Awaiting user input

The running configuration is a single file in RAM that the IOS parses and presents as the nested mode hierarchy. Each `configure terminal` session edits this in-memory file.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Command Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Category | Command | Mode Required | Function |
|----------|---------|---------------|----------|
| Mode Navigation | `enable` | User EXEC | Enter Privileged EXEC |
| Mode Navigation | `disable` | Privileged EXEC | Return to User EXEC |
| Mode Navigation | `configure terminal` | Privileged EXEC | Enter Global Config |
| Mode Navigation | `exit` or `end` (Ctrl+Z) | Any Config | Return to parent mode |
| Mode Navigation | `end` | Any Config | Jump directly to Privileged EXEC |
| Hostname | `hostname <name>` | Global Config | Set router name |
| Password | `enable secret <pwd>` | Global Config | Set encrypted privileged password |
| Banner | `banner motd #message#` | Global Config | Set login banner |
| Interface IP | `ip address <IP> <mask>` | Interface Config | Assign IP to interface |
| Interface Status | `no shutdown` | Interface Config | Enable (turn ON) the interface |
| Interface Status | `shutdown` | Interface Config | Administratively disable interface |
| Description | `description <text>` | Interface Config | Add a label to interface |
| Save Config | `copy running-config startup-config` | Privileged EXEC | Save to NVRAM (also `wr mem`) |
| View Config | `show running-config` | Privileged EXEC | Display active config |
| View Config | `show startup-config` | Privileged EXEC | Display saved config |
| Routing Table | `show ip route` | Privileged EXEC | Display routing table |
| Interfaces | `show ip interface brief` | Privileged EXEC | Show all interfaces summary |
| Interfaces | `show interfaces <int>` | Privileged EXEC | Detailed interface info |
| IOS Info | `show version` | Privileged EXEC | IOS version, uptime, model |
| Console | `line console 0` | Global Config | Enter console line config |
| Remote | `line vty 0 4` | Global Config | Enter VTY (telnet/SSH) config |
| Console Login | `password <pwd>` + `login` | Line Config | Set console authentication |
| Clock | `clock rate <bps>` | Interface Config (Serial DCE) | Set DCE clocking |
| Verify | `ping <ip>` | User/Privileged EXEC | Test connectivity |
| Verify | `traceroute <ip>` | User/Privileged EXEC | Trace path to destination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-frequently-tested commands in KTU 2024 Scheme Lab Exam:** `show ip route`, `show ip interface brief`, `show running-config`, `enable secret`, `ip address`, `no shutdown`, `copy running-config startup-config`, `show version`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Router command familiarity is the **foundational skill** for:
- **Network Engineer (CCNA/CCNP/CCIE tracks)** — daily job involves CLI
- **ISP Edge Router Management** — BGP/OSPF configuration
- **Enterprise LAN/WAN Design** — VLAN, routing, ACL
- **Cybersecurity** — Router hardening, AAA, firewall ACLs
- **Cloud Networking** — Cisco CSR 1000v, IOS-XE in AWS/Azure
- **DevOps NetOps** — Ansible/Chef automation of IOS commands
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Command Workflows & Analytical Matrix

This section provides complete, step-by-step execution flows for the most common KTU lab exam tasks on this topic.

## 3.1 Workflow A: Initial Router Configuration (Hostname, Passwords, Banner)

**Scenario:** You power on a router named `Router` and must configure it with hostname `KTU-R1`, an encrypted privileged secret `cisco123`, and a Message-of-the-Day banner.

### Step-by-Step Execution

```text
Step 1:  Router>                          ← Initial prompt (User EXEC)
         Router> enable
Step 2:  Router#                          ← Privileged EXEC
         Router# configure terminal
Step 3:  Router(config)#                  ← Global Configuration
         Router(config)# hostname KTU-R1
Step 4:  KTU-R1(config)#                  ← Hostname changed
         KTU-R1(config)# enable secret cisco123
Step 5:  KTU-R1(config)# banner motd #Authorized Access Only#
Step 6:  KTU-R1(config)# end
Step 7:  KTU-R1#                          ← Back to Privileged EXEC
         KTU-R1# copy running-config startup-config
              Destination filename [startup-config]? <Enter>
              Building configuration...
              [OK]
Step 8:  KTU-R1#
```

### Detailed Explanation of Each Step

**Step 1 — Initial State:** When a router boots with no startup configuration, it runs the **Setup Mode** wizard. We bypass it by typing `no` to the initial dialogue. The router lands in User EXEC with the default hostname `Router`.

**Step 2 — `enable`:** Promotes the session from User EXEC to Privileged EXEC. No password is required if none has been set; otherwise, the enable secret/password is prompted.

**Step 3 — `configure terminal`:** Enters Global Configuration. The `terminal` keyword means the source of commands is the current terminal (as opposed to a network file like TFTP). The `conf t` abbreviation also works.

**Step 4 — `hostname KTU-R1`:** Changes the device name. The change is immediately reflected in the prompt. This command resides in the running configuration and persists after a `copy run start`.

**Step 5 — `enable secret cisco123`:** Sets the privileged EXEC password using **MD5 hashing** (Type 5). This is more secure than the legacy `enable password` command (Type 7 reversible encryption, deprecated in modern IOS).

**Step 6 — `banner motd #...#`:** The `motd` (Message of the Day) banner is displayed **before** the login prompt. The `#` is the delimiter; it can be any character that does not appear in the message.

**Step 7 — `end` (or `Ctrl+Z`):** Exits any configuration mode and returns directly to Privileged EXEC. This is faster than repeated `exit` commands.

**Step 8 — `copy running-config startup-config`:** Saves the in-memory active config (RAM) to NVRAM. The running-config is volatile — lost on reload. Without this step, all configuration is lost on reboot. The shortcut is `wr` (write) or `wr mem`.

## 3.2 Workflow B: Interface IP Configuration and Verification

**Scenario:** Configure `GigabitEthernet0/0` with IP `192.168.1.1/24` and `Serial0/0/0` with IP `10.0.0.1/30`. Set the serial clock rate. Verify with `show` commands.

### Step-by-Step Execution

```text
Step 1:  Router> enable
Step 2:  Router# configure terminal
Step 3:  Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
Step 4:  Router(config-if)# ip address 192.168.1.1 255.255.255.0
Step 5:  Router(config-if)# description LAN Connection to PC1
Step 6:  Router(config-if)# no shutdown
Step 7:  Router(config-if)# exit
Step 8:  Router(config)# interface Serial0/0/0
Step 9:  Router(config-if)# ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.252
Step 10: Router(config-if)# clock rate 64000
Step 11: Router(config-if)# no shutdown
Step 12: Router(config-if)# end
Step 13: Router# show ip interface brief
```

### Expected Output of `show ip interface brief`

```text
Interface              IP-Address      OK? Method Status                Protocol
GigabitEthernet0/0     192.168.1.1     YES manual up                    up
GigabitEthernet0/1     unassigned      YES NVRAM  administratively down down
Serial0/0/0            10.0.0.1        YES manual up                    up
Serial0/0/1            unassigned      YES NVRAM  administratively down down
```

### Step-by-Step Verification Logic

**Verification Column 1 — `IP-Address`:** Displays the configured IPv4 address. `unassigned` means no IP was set (or was removed with `no ip address`).

**Verification Column 2 — `OK?`:** `YES` means the IP address is valid. `NO` indicates a duplicate IP or other error.

**Verification Column 3 — `Method`:** Indicates how the IP was obtained. `manual` = static configuration, `NVRAM` = from startup-config, `DHCP` = dynamically assigned.

**Verification Column 4 — `Status`:** Layer 1 (physical) status. `up` = cable connected and pinout valid, `administratively down` = interface is shut down by config (`shutdown` command), `down` = cable issue or remote end down.

**Verification Column 5 — `Protocol`:** Layer 2 (data link) status. `up` = keepalives being received. If `Status` is up but `Protocol` is down, suspect encapsulation or clocking mismatch on serial links.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** `Status up/up` = working. `Status down/down` = physical issue. `Status administratively down/down` = needs `no shutdown`.

## 3.3 Workflow C: Reading and Interpreting the Routing Table

The output of `show ip route` is critical for KTU lab viva. Here is a sample output with a detailed annotation matrix.

### Sample Output

```text
Router# show ip route
Codes: C - connected, S - static, I - IGRP, R - RIP, M - mobile, B - BGP
       D - EIGRP, EX - EIGRP external, O - OSPF, IA - OSPF inter area
       N1 - OSPF NSSA external type 1, N2 - OSPF NSSA external type 2
       E1 - OSPF external type 1, E2 - OSPF external type 2
       i - IS-IS, su - IS-IS summary, L1 - IS-IS level-1, L2 - IS-IS level-2
       ia - IS-IS inter area, * - candidate default, U - per-user static route
       o - ODR, P - periodic downloaded static route, H - NHRP, l - LISP
       a - application route
       + - replicated route, % - next hop override

Gateway of last resort is not set

     10.0.0.0/8 is variably subnetted, 2 subnets, 2 masks
C       10.0.0.0/30 is directly connected, Serial0/0/0
L       10.0.0.1/32 is directly connected, Serial0/0/0
     192.168.1.0/24 is variably subnetted, 2 subnets, 2 masks
C       192.168.1.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0
L       192.168.1.1/32 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0
R    172.16.0.0/24 [120/1] via 10.0.0.2, 00:00:15, Serial0/0/0
```

### Routing Table Entry Decoding Matrix

| Position | Value | Meaning |
|----------|-------|---------|
| Code `C` | Connected | Network is directly attached to a router interface |
| Code `L` | Local | IOS automatically adds /32 host route for the interface IP itself |
| Code `S` | Static | Manually configured static route |
| Code `R` | RIP | Learned via RIP routing protocol |
| Code `D` | EIGRP | Learned via EIGRP |
| Code `O` | OSPF | Learned via OSPF |
| Network | `172.16.0.0/24` | Destination network and prefix length |
| Administrative Distance | `[120/...]` | Trustworthiness of the source (RIP=120, OSPF=110, EIGRP=90, Static=1, Connected=0) |
| Metric | `[.../1]` | Path cost; lower is better |
| Next Hop | `via 10.0.0.2` | IP address of the next router to forward to |
| Timer | `00:00:15` | Time since last update (in h:mm:ss) |
| Outgoing Interface | `Serial0/0/0` | Local interface used to reach next hop |

### Key Routing Table Codes for KTU Exam

| Code | Protocol | Default AD |
|------|----------|------------|
| C | Connected | 0 |
| L | Local | 0 |
| S | Static | 1 |
| D | EIGRP | 90 (Internal) |
| O | OSPF | 110 |
| R | RIP | 120 |
| B | BGP | 20 (eBGP) / 200 (iBGP) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode vs. Allowed Commands

| Command Category | User EXEC | Priv EXEC | Global Config | Interface Config |
|------------------|:---------:|:---------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|
| `ping` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `show ip route` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `show running-config` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `configure terminal` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `hostname` | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `enable secret` | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| `ip address` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `no shutdown` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `description` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| `copy run start` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `reload` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| `write erase` | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** A common mistake is trying to issue `ip address` directly at `Router#`. It will only work inside `Router(config-if)#`. The IOS error will be: `% Invalid input detected at '^' marker`.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Router Mode Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Router greater than User EXEC Mode"]
    B["Router hash Privileged EXEC Mode"]
    C["Router config hash Global Configuration"]
    D["Router config-if hash Interface Configuration"]
    E["Router config-subif hash Sub Interface Configuration"]
    F["Router config-line hash Line Configuration"]
    G["Router config-router hash Router Configuration"]

    A -- enable command --> B
    B -- configure terminal command --> C
    C -- interface fa0 slash 0 --> D
    C -- interface fa0 slash 0 dot 10 --> E
    C -- line console 0 --> F
    C -- router rip --> G

    D -- exit --> C
    E -- exit --> C
    F -- exit --> C
    G -- exit --> C
    C -- end command --> B
    B -- disable command --> A

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF8C42,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#E07A35,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#C1666B,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#5E8C61,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#3D5A80,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Packet Forwarding Logic with Routing Table (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P["Incoming IP Packet arrives on interface Gi0 slash 0"]
    Q["Extract destination IP"]
    R["Longest Prefix Match against Routing Table"]
    S{"Match Found?"}
    T["Use route next hop and outgoing interface"]
    U["Decrement TTL and recompute checksum"]
    V["Forward packet out egress interface"]
    W["Drop packet and send ICMP Destination Unreachable"]

    P --> Q
    Q --> R
    R --> S
    S -- YES --> T
    T --> U
    U --> V
    S -- NO --> W

    style P fill:#A8DADC,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#F1FAEE,stroke:#333
    style S fill:#E63946,stroke:#fff
    style V fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#fff
    style W fill:#EF476F,stroke:#fff
```

## 4.3 Configuration Save & Boot Process (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["RAM Running Config Volatile"]
    B["copy running-config startup-config"]
    C["NVRAM Startup Config Non-volatile"]
    D["Reload or Power Cycle"]
    E["IOS reads NVRAM"]
    F["RAM Running Config restored"]

    A -- B --> C
    C -- D --> E
    E -- F --> A

    style A fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#fff
    style B fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#fff
    style D fill:#1A535C,stroke:#fff
    style E fill:#1A535C,stroke:#fff
    style F fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Typical Lab Topology with Router Interfaces (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC1["PC1<br/>192.168.1.10 slash 24"]
    PC2["PC2<br/>192.168.2.10 slash 24"]
    R1["Router R1<br/>Gi0/0: 192.168.1.1 slash 24<br/>Se0/0/0: 10.0.0.1 slash 30"]
    R2["Router R2<br/>Se0/0/0: 10.0.0.2 slash 30<br/>Gi0/0: 192.168.2.1 slash 24"]

    PC1 -- FastEthernet --> R1
    R1 -- Serial DCE clock 64000 --> R2
    R2 -- FastEthernet --> PC2

    style R1 fill:#FF8C42,stroke:#fff
    style R2 fill:#3D5A80,stroke:#fff
    style PC1 fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#fff
    style PC2 fill:#06D6A0,stroke:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram represents the canonical 2-router, 2-PC topology used in the KTU PCCSL507 lab for Module Experiment 2. Students are expected to assign IPs, set DCE clocking on `Serial0/0/0` of R1, and verify end-to-end connectivity using `ping` and `show ip route`.
