| Command: Request | **1** | Full table request |
| Command: Response | **2** | Periodic + triggered |
| Version | **1** | Always 1 for RIPng |
| Max Hop Count | **15** | 16 = unreachable |
| Update Interval | **30 s** | Full periodic update |
| Invalid Timer | **180 s** | Marks route as "possibly down" |
| Hold-Down Timer | **180 s** | Suppresses updates during instability |
| Flush Timer | **240 s** | Removes route from table |
| Administrative Distance | **120** | Cisco default; same as RIPv1/v2 |
| Max RTE per Message | **25** | RFC 2080 limit |
| AFIs supported | **IPv6 only** | AFI field removed; IPv6 is implicit |
| Authentication | **None** (relies on IPsec) | Frequently asked in viva |
| Default Route Injection | `default-information originate` | Distributes ::/0 |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Campus and small enterprise networks** where simplicity outweighs scalability.
- **IPv6 transition training labs** (the topic of this KTU experiment).
- **Educational environments** — RIPng is still preferred in academic labs (including KTU PCCSL507) because of its low configuration overhead and high pedagogical value.
- **Loopback and lab backbones** where OSPFv3 or EIGRP for IPv6 would be over-engineered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality (2024–2026)** : In production ISPs and large enterprises, RIPng has been **completely replaced by OSPFv3, IS-IS for IPv6, MP-BGP, or EIGRP for IPv6**. RIPng is now considered a **legacy / educational protocol**, but remains in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for foundational exposure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — When does RIPng prefer a route?

Given two candidate paths to the same IPv6 prefix, RIPng picks the one with the **lower metric after adding the local hop (cost-of-arrival)**. Formally, for a candidate entry received from neighbour *N* advertising prefix *P* with metric *m*:

$$\text{Advertised\_Cost} = m$$

$$\text{Local\_Cost} = m + 1$$

A route is installed if:

$$\text{Local\_Cost} < 16 \quad \wedge \quad \text{Local\_Cost} < \text{Current\_Metric}(P)$$

If `Local_Cost = 16`, the route is **marked invalid (unreachable)** — this is the **Bellman–Ford infinity condition** that gives RIPng its loop-prevention ceiling.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Three-Router Convergence

**Topology (the canonical KTU lab diagram):**

- R1 ↔ R2 via link `2001:DB8:12::/64`
- R2 ↔ R3 via link `2001:DB8:23::/64`
- R1 LAN = `2001:DB8:1::/64`
- R2 LAN = `2001:DB8:2::/64`
- R3 LAN = `2001:DB8:3::/64`

**Step-by-step Bellman–Ford propagation of `2001:DB8:3::/64` (R3's LAN):**

Let the symbol `D_X(Y)` denote **X's current distance to destination Y**.

**Iteration 0 (initial state, only directly connected):**
- `D_R1(R3_LAN)` = 16 (unknown)
- `D_R2(R3_LAN)` = 16 (unknown)
- `D_R3(R3_LAN)` = 0 (directly connected)

**Iteration 1 (after 1 × 30 s update cycle):**

R3 multicasts to FF02::9:
- `2001:DB8:3::/64, metric = 1`

R2 receives, computes `1 + 1 = 2`. Installs route:
- `D_R2(R3_LAN) = 2`, next-hop = R3's link-local on the 2001:DB8:23:: link.

R2 then multicasts its own table (with metric = 3 for 2001:DB8:3::/64 because of the +1 cost from R2 to R3).

R1 receives, computes `3 + 1 = 4`. Installs:
- `D_R1(R3_LAN) = 4`, next-hop = R2's link-local on the 2001:DB8:12:: link.

**Iteration 2 (after 2 × 30 s, network stable):**

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{R1}(R3\_LAN) &= 4 \\
D_{R2}(R3\_LAN) &= 2 \\
D_{R3}(R3\_LAN) &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Convergence reached.** Total convergence time for a 3-router chain = `2 × 30 s = 60 s`. Maximum worst case for 15-hop diameter = `15 × 30 s = 7.5 min`.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Cisco IOS Configuration Walk-through

**Step 1 — Enable IPv6 unicast routing (mandatory; RIPng will silently fail otherwise):**

```
Router(config)# ipv6 unicast-routing
```

**Step 2 — Configure IPv6 addresses on every active interface:**

```
Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
Router(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
Router(config-if)# no shutdown
```

**Step 3 — Define the RIPng process (process name is local-only, NOT exchanged):**

```
Router(config)# ipv6 router rip KTU-RIPNG
```

> [!NOTE]
> `KTU-RIPNG` is a **locally significant tag** used only on the local router; it is **not** advertised in updates. Different routers may use different tags.

**Step 4 — Enable RIPng on each participating interface:**

```
Router(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
Router(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU-RIPNG enable
```

**Step 5 — (Optional) Distribute a default route:**

```
Router(config-rtr)# default-information originate
```

**Step 6 — Verification (mandatory for full KTU viva marks):**

```
Router# show ipv6 rip
Router# show ipv6 route rip
Router# show ipv6 protocols
Router# debug ipv6 rip
```

### 3.4 Decision Table — When to Enable RIPng on an Interface

| Interface Type | Enable RIPng? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| LAN interface to internal segment | **Yes** | Network to be advertised |
| WAN link to other RIPng router | **Yes** | Forms the dynamic mesh |
| Loopback (for management) | Optional | Useful for testing |
| Interface connected to non-RIPng domain | **No** | Prevents info leakage |
| Interface facing the public Internet | **No** | Security: prevents RIPng ingress |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Network Topology (Canonical KTU Lab Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pc1["PC1\n2001:DB8:1::10/64"]
    r1["R1\nG0/0 2001:DB8:1::1/64\nG0/1 2001:DB8:12::1/64"]
    r2["R2\nG0/0 2001:DB8:2::1/64\nG0/1 2001:DB8:12::2/64\nG0/2 2001:DB8:23::1/64"]
    r3["R3\nG0/0 2001:DB8:3::1/64\nG0/1 2001:DB8:23::2/64"]
    pc2["PC2\n2001:DB8:2::10/64"]
    pc3["PC3\n2001:DB8:3::10/64"]

    pc1 --- r1
    r1 --- r2
    r2 --- r3
    pc2 --- r2
    pc3 --- r3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — RIPng Operational Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Router boots and ipv6 unicast-routing is enabled"] --> B["ipv6 rip TAG enable issued on interface"]
    B --> C["Router sends RIPng Request to FF02::9 UDP 521"]
    C --> D{"Neighbour RIPng\nspeaker alive?"}
    D -- No --> E["Route remains unknown; metric stays 16"]
    D -- Yes --> F["Receive Response with RTEs"]
    F --> G["Compute Local_Cost = Received_Metric + 1"]
    G --> H{"Local_Cost less than 16\nAND less than current metric?"}
    H -- No --> I["Discard or mark invalid"]
    H -- Yes --> J["Install route with next-hop = source link-local"]
    J --> K["Wait 30 s then multicast own table to FF02::9"]
    K --> F
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — RIPng Packet Format (RTE Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    H["Header (4 bytes)\nCommand=1 or 2\nVersion=1\nReserved=0"]
    R1["RTE 1\nPrefix 16B\nTag 2B\nLen 1B\nMetric 1B"]
    R2["RTE 2\nPrefix 16B\nTag 2B\nLen 1B\nMetric 1B"]
    R3["RTE n (up to 25)"]
    H --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Header** is fixed at **4 bytes**. Each **RTE** is **20 bytes**. Maximum payload = `4 + 25 × 20 = 504 bytes`, fitting comfortably within a single IPv6 link-local multicast datagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, PCCSL507]**
*List the four key differences between RIPv2 (IPv4) and RIPng (IPv6).*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid point, max 3):**
1. RIPv2 uses **IPv4 addressing and broadcasts (255.255.255.255) on UDP 520**; RIPng uses **IPv6 addressing and multicasts to FF02::9 on UDP 521**.
2. RIPv2 supports **authentication (plain-text or MD5)** in its message; RIPng has **no built-in authentication** and relies on **IPsec** at the network layer.
3. RIPv2 RTE carries the **AFI (Address Family Identifier)** field explicitly; RIPng has **no AFI** because IPv6 is the only supported family.
4. RIPv2 includes a **next-hop field** in the RTE; RIPng's next hop is **implicitly the source link-local address** of the advertising router (no explicit next-hop field).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, PCCSL507]**
*Why is the maximum hop count in RIPng set to 15, and what is the value that represents "unreachable"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The maximum usable hop count is **15** because RIPng uses the **Bellman–Ford distance-vector algorithm**, which is highly susceptible to **routing loops** as the diameter increases. Capping the diameter at 15 hops guarantees that any "count-to-infinity" loop terminates within a bounded time. The value **16** is reserved as the **"infinity" or "unreachable"** marker — a route advertised with metric 16 is treated as invalid. This 15/16 split is mandated by **RFC 2080** and is a direct inheritance from RIPv1/RIPv2.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** — Marks are routinely lost in three areas: (1) Forgetting to execute `ipv6 unicast-routing` in global config (RIPng updates are silently dropped), (2) Mistyping the IPv6 address syntax (using a colon where a double-colon is required for zero-compression), and (3) Failing to specify the correct RIPng tag on **every** participating interface, leading to asymmetric updates.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **RIPng packet format** and the **operation of the protocol** using a request-response cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** Block diagram showing: 4-byte header (Command: 1=Request, 2=Response; Version: 1; Reserved: 2 bytes) followed by **1 to 25 RTEs**, each 20 bytes (16-byte IPv6 prefix, 2-byte route tag, 1-byte prefix length, 1-byte metric).
- **(2 marks)** When a router starts, it sends a **Request (Command=1)** to FF02::9. All neighbours reply with **Response (Command=2)** messages containing their full routing tables.
- **(2 marks)** Periodic operation: every 30 s, each router multicasts its full table. Triggered updates are sent immediately upon topology change.
- **(1 mark)** The metric is recomputed as `received_metric + 1`; routes with metric ≥ 16 are discarded as invalid.

**(b)** Design and configure a **three-router IPv6 network** using RIPng. Router **R1** has LAN `2001:DB8:1::/64`, **R2** connects R1 and R3 with networks `2001:DB8:12::/64` and `2001:DB8:23::/64`, and **R3** has LAN `2001:DB8:3::/64`. Provide the **complete Cisco IOS configuration** for all three routers and the **commands to verify the RIPng table** on R1. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**R1 Configuration (2 marks):**
```
R1(config)# ipv6 unicast-routing
R1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
R1(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64
R1(config-if)# no shutdown
R1(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU enable
R1(config-if)# exit
R1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1
R1(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:12::1/64
R1(config-if)# no shutdown
R1(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU enable
```

**R2 Configuration (2 marks):**
```
R2(config)# ipv6 unicast-routing
R2(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
R2(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:12::2/64
R2(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU enable
R2(config-if)# exit
R2(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1
R2(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:23::1/64
R2(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU enable
```

**R3 Configuration (1 mark):**
```
R3(config)# ipv6 unicast-routing
R3(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0
R3(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:23::2/64
R3(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU enable
R3(config-if)# exit
R3(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1
R3(config-if)# ipv6 address 2001:DB8:3::1/64
R3(config-if)# ipv6 rip KTU enable
```

**Verification on R1 (2 marks):**
```
R1# show ipv6 protocols           ! confirms RIPng process is running
R1# show ipv6 rip                 ! shows RIPng-specific timers and interfaces
R1# show ipv6 route rip           ! lists the IPv6 routes learned via RIPng
R1# show ipv6 interface brief     ! confirms link-local and global addresses
```

The expected RIB on R1 should include:
- `R 2001:DB8:2::/64 [120/2]` via `FE80::2`, GigabitEthernet0/1
- `R 2001:DB8:3::/64 [120/3]` via `FE80::2`, GigabitEthernet0/1
- `C 2001:DB8:1::/64 [0/1]` directly connected
- `C 2001:DB8:12::/64 [0/1]` directly connected

The `[120/x]` notation is the **administrative distance / metric** — always **120** for RIPng on Cisco IOS.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **four timers** used by RIPng (Update, Invalid, Hold-down, Flush) with their default values and operational significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks — distribute ~1.75 marks per timer):**

1. **Update Timer (30 s)** — Controls the periodic multicast of the full routing table to FF02::9. After expiry, the router sends an unsolicited Response to all RIPng-enabled interfaces. This is the **heartbeat** of the protocol.

2. **Invalid Timer (180 s)** — If no update is received for a route within 180 s, the route is marked as **"possibly down"** and its metric is set to 16 (infinity). The route remains in the routing table but is no longer used for forwarding.

3. **Hold-Down Timer (180 s)** — When a route becomes invalid, the router **refuses to accept updates** for that destination for 180 s. This prevents a flapping link from re-injecting a bad route back into the network before convergence. (1 mark)

4. **Flush Timer (240 s)** — After 240 s (60 s longer than the Invalid timer), the route is **completely purged** from the routing table. This guarantees that stale entries do not accumulate forever. (1 mark)

The relationship is: `Flush = Invalid + 60 s`. This 60-second overlap is precisely the hold-down window in which the route is marked invalid but not yet flushed.

**(b)** Demonstrate the **route convergence** in a 4-router linear topology (R1—R2—R3—R4) where R4 advertises its LAN `2001:DB8:4::/64`. Show the metric evolution at R1, R2, and R3 after the first three update cycles. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Cycle 0 (t = 0 s):** All routers hold direct cost = 0 to their own LAN; cost to `2001:DB8:4::/64` = 16 (unknown) for R1, R2, R3; cost = 0 for R4.

**Cycle 1 (t = 30 s):** R4 multicasts `2001:DB8:4::/64, metric 1`.
- R3 receives → installs metric 2.
- R3 advertises to R2 with metric 3.
- R2 advertises to R1 with metric 4.
- R1 receives → installs metric 5.

**Cycle 2 (t = 60 s):** Network stabilizes with:
- `D_R4 = 0`
- `D_R3 = 2`
- `D_R2 = 3`
- `D_R1 = 5`

**Cycle 3 (t = 90 s):** Same as Cycle 2 — **convergence achieved** in `3 × 30 s = 90 s` (because R4's first advertisement must traverse 3 hops, each adding 30 s of gossip latency).

Tabulated summary (1 mark):

| Time | D_R1 | D_R2 | D_R3 | D_R4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 s | 16 | 16 | 16 | 0 |
| 30 s | 5 | 4 | 2 | 0 |
| 60 s | 5 | 3 | 2 | 0 |
| 90 s | 5 | 3 | 2 | 0 |

This shows the **counting-to-1** property: R1's metric stabilizes at 4 (hops) + 1 (its own link) = **5**, R2 stabilizes at 2 + 1 = **3**, R3 stabilizes at 1 + 1 = **2**, R4 at **0**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PCCSL507 Module 2 Viva**

- **RIPng = RIPv2 for IPv6**, standardized in **RFC 2080**, runs over **UDP 521**.
- Uses **multicast FF02::9** at link-local scope — **never** routed; **never** uses broadcast.
- **No authentication** in the protocol itself; must rely on **IPsec AH/ESP**.
- **Maximum hop count = 15**; **metric 16 = infinity** (unreachable).
- **Administrative Distance on Cisco = 120**; same as RIPv1/RIPv2.
- **Update interval = 30 s**; full routing table is sent every cycle.
- **Timers** : Update 30 s, Invalid 180 s, Hold-down 180 s, Flush 240 s.
- **Cisco IOS essentials** : `ipv6 unicast-routing` is **mandatory**; `ipv6 rip TAG enable` must be applied on **every** participating interface.
- **Process tag is local-only** — it is NOT exchanged between routers.
- **Next-hop is implicit** = the source link-local address of the advertising router.
- **Max 25 RTEs per message**; each RTE is 20 bytes; header is 4 bytes.
- **Loop prevention** : Split horizon, poison reverse, hold-down timers, triggered updates, and the 15-hop cap.
- **Verification commands** (memorize): `show ipv6 rip`, `show ipv6 route rip`, `show ipv6 protocols`, `debug ipv6 rip`.
- **Real-world use (2024–2026)** : Educational and lab environments only; production networks have moved to **OSPFv3, IS-IS, MP-BGP, or EIGRP for IPv6**.
- **Common viva question** : *"Why doesn't RIPng include a next-hop field like RIPv2?"* — Because the source link-local address of the IPv6 packet is already a globally unique next-hop identifier within the local segment, eliminating redundancy.
- **Common lab mistake** : Forgetting `ipv6 unicast-routing` results in zero updates being sent; the symptom is `show ipv6 rip` showing an empty database.
